Thyroid Gland

Thyroid Gland
  
• A ductless endocrine gland situated in the anterior / front portion of the neck. 
• Thyroid gland roughly resembles the shape of a butterﬂy. 
• Also marked as one of the largest endocrine glands, weighing an average of 25 – 30 grams. 
• This gland has two lobes on either side of the trachea; each lobe measuring 4 – 6 cm in length 
and 1.3 – 1.8 cm in width.
• The primary function of the thyroid gland is to secrete two hormones; Triiodothyronine (T3) 
hormone and the Thyroxine hormone (T4). 
• Both T3 and T4 hormones play a very important role and affect almost every tissue in the 
body.

Thyroid Gland - Functions
• Regulates the metabolic rate of the body.
• Also regulates the metabolism of fat, proteins and carbohydrates.
• Thyroid hormones help with brain development and cognitive functions.
• It also helps with muscle control as well as bone health.
• Thyroid hormones also help with protein synthesis.
• Increase the body’s sensitivity to catecholami

n development and cognitive functions.
• It also helps with muscle control as well as bone health.
• Thyroid hormones also help with protein synthesis.
• Increase the body’s sensitivity to catecholamines.
• Essential for the development and differentiation of cells.

Thyroid Gland – Hormones (TSH, T3 & T4)
TSH: 
• Thyroid Stimulating Hormone is a messenger released by the 
pituitary gland. 
• It plays the role of the master of the hormones, and rules the 
production of T3 and T4 from its control center.
Triiodothyronine (T3):
• T3 is produced by the thyroid gland as well as in other tissues, via 
the removal of iodine from Thyroxine (T4).
Thyroxine (T4):
• T4 is also produced by the thyroid gland under the regulation of 
the pituitary gland and the hypothalamus. 
• It is secreted into the bloodstream and travels to organs such as 
the kidneys and liver. Here, thyroxine is converted into its active 
form – triiodothyronine.

Hypothyroidism
• A condition in which the thyroid gland doesn't produce enough  thyroid hormone.
• Thyroid gland secrete T3, T4 hormones which are responsible for  metabolism, BMR, nutrient uptake, hemoglobin 
production,  alertness, growth and reproduction regulation etc. In absence or  lowered levels of these hormones which 
are triggered for secretion  by TSH (released via pituitary), major body functions are slowed  down.
Major symptoms: 
• Weight gain
• Fatigue
• Constipation
• Fertility issues
• Menstrual disturbances
• Muscle weakness
• hair loss
• Decreased kidney function
• Treatment consists of medication with synthetic thyroid hormone replacement in form of tablets (Thyroxine) taken on 
an empty  stomach in the morning (hormone replacement therapy).
• The dosage varies as per the level detect

dication with synthetic thyroid hormone replacement in form of tablets (Thyroxine) taken on 
an empty  stomach in the morning (hormone replacement therapy).
• The dosage varies as per the level detected in T3 T4 TSH blood  work.

Dealing with Hypothyroidism
• Thyroid hormones are responsible for a lot of metabolism related processes and pathways. 
• Falling short of the hormone via its natural route often leaves a stunted or slowed down response 
to showing results. 
• Supplementation to make sure thyroid levels are normalized is the only option people are left 
with more often. 
• Since body’s metabolism is slowed down, often people tend to pile on more weight; this is a loop – 
weight is put on and metabolism is stunted too to make sure weight is not lost. 
• With such cases, setting up a realistic approach while ﬁxing nutrition along with workout will be 
the way out. 
• Lifestyle changes can help best combat such lifestyle disorders.

Hyperthyroidism
• A condition in which the thyroid gland produces too much thyroid hormones (T3 and T4).
• Mostly caused by auto immune disease called Graves Disease. 
• Excessive Iodine intake can also cause Hyperthyroidism.
Major Symptoms:
• Fatigue
• Difﬁculty sleeping
• Weight loss
• Enlarged thyroid, in form of swelling in neck
• Heart palpitation
• Hair loss
• Irritability

Dealing with Hyperthyroidism
• Anti thyroid drugs
• Radio active Iodine Ablation: RAI treats hyperthyroidism by damaging or destroying 
thyroid cells through radiation thereby reducing concentration of iodine in gland 
which is a precursor to T3-T4 synthesis.
• Surgery

Thyroidectomy
• Surgical removal of all or some part of the thyroid gland. 
• Performed to treat thyroid disorders (cancer, hyperthyroidism, goiter, thyroid nodules). 
• If only some part of th

rgery

Thyroidectomy
• Surgical removal of all or some part of the thyroid gland. 
• Performed to treat thyroid disorders (cancer, hyperthyroidism, goiter, thyroid nodules). 
• If only some part of thyroid needs to be removed (partial thyroidectomy), the remaining portion 
typically takes over the function of the entire thyroid gland and does not call for any hormone 
replacement therapy. 
• If entire thyroid is removed, then hormone replacement therapy is required in order to provide 
thyroid hormone which replaces thyroid’s natural function. The body would undergo the state of 
hypothyroidism (underactive thyroid) in case of no medication.
• Thyroidectomy is a safe procedure generally; some associated risks like bleeding, infection,

Vitamins & Minerals

Important parameters for an overall health
• Vitamins, Minerals are an important aspect of overall well being of human body. 
• As much as we need macronutritients for our growth and development, any up and down with the 
micros can cause a subdued result to our performance and output at the same time. 
• Water on the other hand is the best detox. Drinking water for day needs is highly under-rated and never 
considered. 
• Oxygen – because we get it so easily, have we ever realised how important it is? Ask a person who cannot 
breathe properly!

Vitamins

Vitamins
• An organic molecule that is an essential micronutrient which we need in trace quantities for proper 
functioning of body’s metabolism. 
• Essential nutrients cannot be synthesized generally, either at all or in sufﬁcient quantities hence their 
requirement must be fulﬁlled through the diet. 
• The important vitamins we need are: 
• Vitamin A (Retinol) – Fat soluble
• Vitamin D (Cholecalciferol) – Fat so

Major Vitamin deﬁciency – Generic 
• Vitamin D: 
o Important for bone health
o Deﬁciency of Vitamin D leads to poor absorption of calcium and softening / weakening of bones.  
o Including fortiﬁed foods apart from an early morning exposure to sun rays is the best medicine to make sure this 
deﬁciency doesn’t hit you. 
• Vitamin B12: 
o This is an important vitamin which aids in production of RBC and DNA, helps in neurotransmittor functionality.
o Vitamin B12 deﬁciency is often marked with starting of numbness in legs, hands, fe

: 
o This is an important vitamin which aids in production of RBC and DNA, helps in neurotransmittor functionality.
o Vitamin B12 deﬁciency is often marked with starting of numbness in legs, hands, feet, tingling sensation, problem with 
body balance, anemia and difﬁculty in concentration. These symptoms are a wide range to even think of Vitamin B12 
deﬁciency at the ﬁrst place. 
o However, ensuring dietary intake with foods rich in Vitamin B12 like ﬁsh, chicken, milk, yoghurt etc. and fortiﬁed foods 
often helps. 
o Supplementation with Vitamin B12 capsules or else injectable in case of extreme low conditions is other way out. 
• Vitamin K:
•  Vitamin K plays an important role in coagulation, also known as blood clotting, which helps prevent excessive bleeding in 
situations. 
• The deﬁciency is often marked in children and young age, rarely in adults by easy bruises and excessive bleeding
• Taking anticoagulants and antibiotics for a prescribed time is the major way of dealing the de

Minerals and Antioxidants

• Minerals are essential for body health, enzyme and hormone functions 
on a major basis. 
Minerals can be divided as:
• Macro-minerals (Sodium, Chloride, Potassium, Calcium, Phosphorous, 
Magnesium, Sulphur) which are required in a larger quantity.
• Trace-minerals (Iron, Zinc, Iodine, Selenium, Copper, Manganese, 
Flouride, Chromium, Molybdenum) which are required in very small 
quantity. 
Antioxidants: 
• These are compounds which inhibit oxidation thereby limiting free 
radicals and chain reactions in body that can damage body cells 
(basically counteracting oxidative stress). 
• Diet rich in fruits and vegetables often helps to keep up the level 
required as they are antioxidant rich.

Major Mineral deﬁciency – Generic 
• Iron: 
o An important element in producing red blood cells. Iron uptake is very essential. 
o Deﬁciency in iron leads to developing Anemia which is marked with fatigue, lethargy,  weakness, shortness of breath, 
brittle nails, headache, cold hands and feet. 
o Eating iron fortiﬁed foods or foods rich in iron like spinach, beetro

g Anemia which is marked with fatigue, lethargy,  weakness, shortness of breath, 
brittle nails, headache, cold hands and feet. 
o Eating iron fortiﬁed foods or foods rich in iron like spinach, beetroot, beans, lentils etc. help in maintaining a desired level 
of iron in human body. 
o There can also be a stage where iron levels appear ok but ferritin (binding part to haemoglobin) is low. 
• Calcium: 
o Maintaining strong bones, nerve functions, muscle functions is often marked by normal calcium levels in body. 
o In absence of or depleted calcium levels, there can be numbness in hands and feet and abnormal heart rhythm as well. 
o Calcium deﬁciency is not obvious in a short term though. 
o Eating dairy based products, cheese, yoghurt, milk etc. are good to include to make sure of meeting calcium needs in 
body. 
• Iodine:

Supplementation

What are dietary supplements?
● Dietary supplements are intended to provide with nutrients which our whole foods cannot 
sufﬁce or are not in sufﬁcient quantities. 
● These are meant to get nutrients which are lacking in regular diet or else the uptake for 
which is restricted in line with food allergies or reactions. 
● Supplements are NOT a replacement.
● Supplements do not get replaced for healthy foods or snacks as well.
● Can NOT be intended to treat, cure, diagnose, or prevent diseases
● Limited to oral delivery

Why take Supplements?
● Primarily you don’t need if your requirements are sufﬁced from a well-balanced diet.
Supplemtation is often done in order to:
● Sufﬁce necessary substances not found in enough quantity in diet (Eg. Omega 3 / ﬁsh oil in case of 
vegetarians)
● Preventing a disease or condition (Eg. Vit. D)
● Boosting Immunity and health (Vit. C, Vit. D, Omega 3 and Omega 6)
● Improving performance (Creatine)
● Reducing any symptoms or conditions (S

rians)
● Preventing a disease or condition (Eg. Vit. D)
● Boosting Immunity and health (Vit. C, Vit. D, Omega 3 and Omega 6)
● Improving performance (Creatine)
● Reducing any symptoms or conditions (Skin, hair and nail health)

Why are supplements so popular in ﬁtness 
industry?
● Part of our psychology
● Belief in needing “extra”
● Belief in treatment (placebo)
● Marketing is effective
● Self improvement is part of our culture
● Incentivization for promotion by elite athletes
● They may deliver on some of their promises

(ALA), Fat burners, meal replacement shakes etc.
● Performance boosting supplement – Creatine, Sodium bicarbonate, Caffeine, Beta-Alanine,

Women health

The unaddressed facts on Women health
• Women – the unsaid key driver of a family with heaps of responsibilities is often left with no time to address 
her  own health and ﬁtness.
• This being said, issues with women health are seeing growth at a fast pace, majorly because they tend to 
overlook  it or else overladen with work and responsibilities.
• It requires attention and proper propagation to ensure the major pillar of family stands strong and ﬁt.
• Women health issues are majorly lifestyle disorders while some require medical intervention.
“Fit women make a ﬁt home”
In the upcoming slides lets discuss the various health related concerns / topics with women

Menstrual Cycle
• The reproductive cycle in humanfemale is called  menstrual 
cycle.
• The ﬁrst menstruation begins at puberty is termed  
menarche.
• Menstruation is repeated at an average interval of about

Endometriosis
• Endometriosis happens when tissue similar to the tissue lining woman's uterus, grows outside of the uterus. 
• The tissue acts like regular uterine tissue which breaks and bleed at the end of the cycle, however this blood has 
nowhere to go thereby making surrounding areas inﬂamed or swollen. 
• While exact causes are not known, immune system disorders, genes or retrograde menstruation are the likely 
causes. 
• Can affect fertility or getting pregnant. 
• The general symptoms being: 
o Back pain during period and severe menstrual cramps
o Pain while pooping or peeing, especially during period
o Unusual heavy bleeding during periods
o Blood in stool or urine
o Diarrhea or constipation
o Painful sex
o Constant fatigue
o Trouble getting pregnant
• Pain medicines and hormone therapy is often given as ﬁrst line of treatment, else surgery to remove the tissue. .

IBS & Gut Health

What is IBS?
•A group of intestinal symptoms that typically occur together; symptoms varying in severity and 
duration for individual.
•IBS is a common intestinal disorder that causes bloating, stomach cramps, constipation or diarrhea.
•A chronic, relapsing and often life-long disorder. 
•There are four subcategories of IBS:
o Mostly diarrhea and abdominal discomfort (IBS-D)
o Mostly constipation and abdominal discomfort (IBS-C)
o Alternating loose stools and constipation with abdominal discomfort (IBS-mixed)
o Undeﬁned subtype (IBS-U) - symptoms vary

Symptoms
Despite being a heterogeneous disorder symptoms typical include: 
• Disordered defecation i.e constipation or diarrhea, which may be 
debilitating. 
• Abdominal discomfort – sharp pain, cramps, bloating, distension, 
fullness etc.
• Non-ulcer dyspepsia or celiac disease, mucus in stools, incomplete 
evacuation being other markers.
• Symptoms unrelated to intestine like pelvic pain, sleep disturbances, 
headache

, 
fullness etc.
• Non-ulcer dyspepsia or celiac disease, mucus in stools, incomplete 
evacuation being other markers.
• Symptoms unrelated to intestine like pelvic pain, sleep disturbances, 
headaches are also noticed in some cases. 
• There are no speciﬁc lab tests or imaging tests for IBS. 
• IBS does not cause any obvious detectable abnormalities in 
digestive system.
• Diagnosis is typically based on symptoms by gastroenterologist - 
Attempts have been made to identify speciﬁc biomarkers.
Diagnosis
Potential Causes
• Overall sensitive colon or immune system.
• Abnormal serotonin levels in the colon, affecting motility and bowel 
movements
• SIBO (Small Intestinal Bacterial overgrowth)
• Caffeine, Stress and Anxiety, Alcohol, certain foods being other potential 
triggers to IBS

y Recommendations in IBS - Low FODMAP Diet
•A low FODMAP diet appears to be more effective than 
standard dietary advice for symptom control in IBS 
•FODMAPS is a group of fermentable carbohydrates. 
FODMAP stands for Fermentable Oligo-, Di-, 
Mono-saccharides and Polyols; these are poorly 
digested by human digestive system.
•Fermented rapidly by bacteria, which can contribute to 
gastrointestinal symptoms in sensitive individuals. 
FODMAPs draw water into the intestine and trigger 
bacterial fermentation in the large intestine. This occurs 
in most people, but only those with sensitive intestines 
have a reaction.
•Low FODMAP diet has clinical efﬁcacy but markedly 
reduces luminal Biﬁdobacterium concentration

•Oligosaccharide - Starch, Dextrin, Cellulose, 
Pectin, Glycogen
•Disaccharide - Sucrose, Lactose, Maltose
•Monosaccharaides - Glucose, fructose, 
galactose
•Polyols -  Sugar, alcohols e.g. Sorbitol

GERD

Introduction
•Gastroesophageal reﬂux disease, or GERD is a digestive disorder that affects the ring of muscle between esophagus and 
stomach.
•The term “gastroesophageal” refers to the stomach and esophagus while Reﬂux means to ﬂow back or return. 
•Gastroesophageal reﬂux is when stomach acid repeatedly ﬂows back into the tube connecting the mouth and stomach 
(esophagus).
What happens in GERD
•The lower esophageal sphinct

o ﬂow back or return. 
•Gastroesophageal reﬂux is when stomach acid repeatedly ﬂows back into the tube connecting the mouth and stomach 
(esophagus).
What happens in GERD
•The lower esophageal sphincter (LES) is a circular band of muscle at the end of esophagus. 
•In normal digestion, it relaxes and opens when food is swallowed. Then it tightens and closes again afterwards.
•Acid reﬂux happens when LES doesn’t tighten or close properly. This allows digestive juices and other contents from 
stomach to rise up into the esophagus.
•This constant backwash of acid irritates the lining of esophagus, often causing it to become inﬂamed.
•Chronic inﬂammation to extremes can cause Esophagitis or esophageal stricture.

Common Symptoms
•The main symptom of GERD is heartburn: Heartburn 
is a discomfort felt around the chest region; 
speciﬁcally a burning sensation. It tends to get worse 
if the person lies down or bends over, and also after 
eating food.
•Upper abdominal or chest pain
•Trouble swal

felt around the chest region; 
speciﬁcally a burning sensation. It tends to get worse 
if the person lies down or bends over, and also after 
eating food.
•Upper abdominal or chest pain
•Trouble swallowing (dysphagia)
•Sensation of a lump in your throat
Other symptoms like Nausea or vomiting, Respiratory 
problems can also occur. 
Dietary Triggers
• High-fat foods
• Spicy foods, onion, ginger, garlic
• Citrus fruit- Orange, Lemons
• Tomato and food containing tomatoes
• Beverages like- coffee, tea
• Soda, other carbonated beverages
Dietary triggers may vary from person to 
person. Close monitoring will help to identify 
the exact GERD triggers.
Who are Prone to GERD
Certain conditions can increase chances of developing 
GERD: 
•Obesity
•IBS
•Pregnancy
•Hiatal hernia 
•Anxiety

• Consuming large meals
• Keeping larger fasting window

• Eating certain types of foods, such as deep f

•Avoiding overeating or overconsumption

•Refraining from having late night snacks
•Quitting or avoiding smoking
•Avoiding Alcohol 
•Losing weight in case of being overweight can help prevent symptoms
•Dietary modiﬁcations: Diet rich in ﬁbre and low in fat is advisable in preventing reﬂux symptoms

Medical reports – Importance 
• As a Coach, your clients will often put faith and share their latest medical reports which they undergo 
through so that you have a better understanding of their health and ﬁtness levels.
• As a Coach, it is out of scope to suggest them corrections or measures for bettering any particular blood 
work, but yes; you surely can have an understanding on aspects of stamina, faitgue, nutrition tweaks etc. 
which you should be making in favor of your clients. 
• We shall discuss few important reports which you should have an understanding about: 
• Lipid Proﬁle 
• Thyroid Report 
• Blood Glucose / HbA1c

Lipid Proﬁle

Lipid Proﬁle 
• A lipid proﬁle also called as lipid panel is a blood tests used to ﬁnd 
abnormalities in lipids (Cholesterol -  High density lipoproteins, Low 
Density Lipoproteins, Very Low Density Lipoproteins and triglycerides).
• Sample type: Blood sample; more often fasting, can be non fasting.
• The analysis is ofte

l -  High density lipoproteins, Low 
Density Lipoproteins, Very Low Density Lipoproteins and triglycerides).
• Sample type: Blood sample; more often fasting, can be non fasting.
• The analysis is often done to monitor and screen risk of cardiovascular 
diseases. At times also taken for associated liver diseases or to monitor 
any other lifestyle changes.  
• The test reads ﬁve different lipids: Total cholesterol (LDL, VLDL, HDL), 
LDL, HDL, VLDL, Triglycerides.
Lipid test is taken generally if person:   
• Is obese
• Is highly inactive or nil physical activity 
• High BP, diabetic or prediabetic 
• Smoking problem 
• Family history of heart diseases

Lipid Proﬁle – what the report says?
• A lipid proﬁle gives the ranges of all parameters calculated while taking the 
test. 
Generally the normal range for an adult is as follows: 
• Total cholesterol: Below 200 mg/dL
• High-density lipoprotein (HDL) cholesterol: Above 50-60 mg/dL
• Low-density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol: Below 100 mg/d

rmal range for an adult is as follows: 
• Total cholesterol: Below 200 mg/dL
• High-density lipoprotein (HDL) cholesterol: Above 50-60 mg/dL
• Low-density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol: Below 100 mg/dL (For people who 
have diabetes: Below 70 mg/dL)
• Triglycerides: Below 150 mg/dL
These ranges are generally varying for age, other medical conditions as well 
and are best read by a doctor. 
• The test generally is classiﬁed as low risk, high risk, or medium / intermediate 
risk depending on where the results come at. 
• High levels do not necessary indicate a diseased condition, but ofcourse it’s an 
alarm to bring it to range. Lifestyle modiﬁcations in terms of dietary intake 
and exercise routine is a great help in setting lipid proﬁle back to normal. Also 
a continued monitoring of lipid proﬁle is a good measure to take forward.

Thyroid function

Thyroid Function test 
• Many of your clients, specially female clients (Almlst 80% of total client base) will 
often complain about having hypothyroidism or hyperthyroidism. 
• As a Coach, it is out of scope to suggest them corrections or measures for 
bettering or normalizing the thyroid hormone levels but we surely can have an 
understanding on the lows and highs of hormones and its implications. 
• Sample type: Blood sample; more often fasting, can be non fasting.
• The analysis is often done to monitor and screen risk of high or low levels of T4 
(Thyroxine), T3 (Triiodothyronine) and TSH. (thyroid stimulating hormone 
secreted by Pituitary Gland). These can be taken separately or as a whole report. 
• The test reads three of these: T3, T4, TSH. 
• The tests often reveal hypothyroidism (Hashimoto’s disease) or hyperthyroidism 
(Grave’s disease) or may be thyroid nodules etc. with further investigations like 
Ultrasound

of these: T3, T4, TSH. 
• The tests often reveal hypothyroidism (Hashimoto’s disease) or hyperthyroidism 
(Grave’s disease) or may be thyroid nodules etc. with further investigations like 
Ultrasound Sonography or Thyroid scan, radioactive iodine test.

Thyroid Function test – what the report says?
Normal range for an adult:
• TSH: 0.40 - 4.50 mIU/mL (milli-international units per 
liter of blood).
• T4: 5.0 – 12.0 ug/dL (micrograms per deciliter of blood)
• T3: 80 - 220 ng/dL (nanograms per deciliter of blood)
• Measurement of Free T3 is also possible, but is often 
not reliable and hence not that helpful.

High TSH, normal thyroid hormone level Subclinical hypothyroidism
Low TSH, high thyroid hormone level Primary hyperthyroidism

Blood Glucose / Hba1c

Blood glucose / Sugar test 
• Many of your clients, specially mid or old aged (young aged as well) will often report to you that they are 
diabetic or prediabetic and need you to consider this fact while developing their plans. 
• As a Coach, it is out of scope to suggest them measures for normalizing blood glucose levels but we surely 
can have an understanding on the lows and highs of sugar and its implications. 
• Sample type: Blood sample; more often fasting, can be non fasting 
(post prandial – 2 hours post meal generally).
• The analysis is often done to monitor and screen risk of high or low 
levels of glucose present in blood. 
• The test reads glucose levels in blood in a fasted or fed state.

s post meal generally).
• The analysis is often done to monitor and screen risk of high or low 
levels of glucose present in blood. 
• The test reads glucose levels in blood in a fasted or fed state. 
• The test often reveals being diabetic or prediabteic and this is an 
important report at all ages of life considering current lifestyle and 
eating habits.

Blood Glucose test – what the report says?
Normal range for an adult in case of fasting sample:
• 70 - 99 mg/dL or 3.9 – 5.5 mmol/L – Normal 
• 100 - 125 mg/dL or 5.6 – 6.9 mmol/L – Pre-diabetic 
• 126 mg/dL (7.0 mmol/L) or higher – Diabetic 
• Being Pre-diabetic is a risk of developing type 2 Diabetes, 
high Blood pressure (Hypertension) or blood lipid 
disorders. Diabetes often results in impaired kidneys and 
eye vision impairment going forward. 
Normal range for an adult in case of non-fasting sample:
•  A blood glucose level of 140–199 mg/dL – Pre-diabetic 
• A blood glucose level of 200 mg/dL and higher – Diabetic

Blood gluco

ing forward. 
Normal range for an adult in case of non-fasting sample:
•  A blood glucose level of 140–199 mg/dL – Pre-diabetic 
• A blood glucose level of 200 mg/dL and higher – Diabetic

Blood glucose / Sugar test – Hba1c 
• A hemoglobin A1c (HbA1c) test measures the amount of blood 
sugar (glucose) attached to hemoglobin.
• It tells an average blood glucose level over a 3 months time 
period and often stands more accurate and preferred by doctors; 
esp.  In case of people who need to monitor their diabetes. The 
tests helps to understand the modiﬁcations required on 
medication dosage. 
The normal ranges in an adult for Hba1c: 
• Hemoglobin A1c level is between 4% and 5.6% - normal
• Hemoglobin A1c levels between 5.7% and 6.4% - prediabetes 
(higher chance of getting diabetes)
• Hemoglobin A1c Levels of 6.5% or higher - diabetes
•  Even if blood sugar levels regular test show more or less normal 
ranges, it is always advisable to take Hba1c to have a better 
monitoring.

Workout Scheduling – Part I

General Fitness Regime
Warm Ups: 
• Focuses on improving functional activities related to ADL before they safely engage into moderate-intensity physical activities to 
improve overall health. 
• Exercises in this phase should be performed at steady- state. 
• Exercises that promote dynamic balance & active ﬂexibility should be introduced as a part of dynamic warm- up.  
Cardiovascular Strength: Improve baseline ﬁtness levels to improve overall health; introduce light walking.
Movement Training: 
• Training movement patterns - Coaches can help clients mobilize their kinetic chain without compromising stability. 
• Emphasis to proper sequencing of movements and control of body’s COG (Centre of Gravity) through normal ROM (range of 
motion) to develop efﬁcient neural pattern.
• Once a client perform the movement patterns effectively while maintaining stable neural posture, COG, and speed; LOAD can be 
added thereafter.
Cardiovascular strength: 
• Enhance aerob

ral pattern.
• Once a client perform the movement patterns effectively while maintaining stable neural posture, COG, and speed; LOAD can be 
added thereafter.
Cardiovascular strength: 
• Enhance aerobic efﬁciency by progressing to increased duration, frequency & intervals further progression being goal based.
Flexibility Training:
• Decreased ﬂexibility is associated with various musculoskeletal injuries, including muscle strain and over-used conditions. 
• Short / tight Muscle is a weak muscle and can often lead to injuries.

Goal Identiﬁcation

Goal Setting
An effective way to set goals is to use the SMART method. Setting realistic goals using the SMART goal setting process can 
help with client adherence, motivation, progress tracking, and reducing relapses.
Speciﬁc 
The goals must speciﬁcally state what is to be accomplished. They must be clear and easy to understand and should not be 
ambiguous.. Example - I would like to “get ﬁt,” client can be more speciﬁc to the goal such as “I want to run a 5K.”
Measurable
Goals must be measurable so that a client can see if he or she is making progress. For example, a goal of “I want to lose 5 
pounds” has a measurable component compared to “I want lose weight,” which is more ambiguous.
 
Attainable 
Goal to be realistically attainable for the

gress. For example, a goal of “I want to lose 5 
pounds” has a measurable component compared to “I want lose weight,” which is more ambiguous.
 
Attainable 
Goal to be realistically attainable for the client. Goal of “losing 30 pounds in 1 month” is not an attainable goal. However, “losing 
0.3 to 0.4 kg a week for or total of 1 to 3 kg in a month,” is attainable.
Relevant
Should be relevant to the particular interests, needs, and abilities of the client. The goal must be one generated by the client, 
not the ﬁtness professional, so that it has enough personal meaning to motivate the client. Example: heavy resistance training 
would not be the best approach 
to training for a half marathon.
Time Bound
Estimated timeline or deadline for completion. Timelines can be both short- and long-term and should help the client stay 
focused. For example, setting small goals to achieve a bigger one.

Importance of Rest & Recovery
• Recovery relates to your nutrition and your sleep/ rest. If you are going to put 100% into your training, you need 
to ensure you put 100% into your recovery.
• Rest can refer to active rest, which can be almost any form of lower-intensity exercise. 
• If you are sore the day after a hard workout, it may be due to an accumulation of metabolic waste (the lactic 
acid and hydrogen ions related to acidosis) in the involved muscle tiss

sity exercise. 
• If you are sore the day after a hard workout, it may be due to an accumulation of metabolic waste (the lactic 
acid and hydrogen ions related to acidosis) in the involved muscle tissue. 
• Elevating your heart rate with low to moderate intensity exercise the day after a high intensity workout can be 
an effective tactic for removing the metabolic waste; these lower-intensity exercise sessions are often called 
regeneration workouts  because they can promote both recovery and muscle growth. 
• A high intensity weight-lifting day can be followed by a yoga class or a bodyweight workout to help promote 
the recovery process. 
• A high intensity cardio day can be followed with lower-intensity exercise like cycling at a moderate tempo or 
walking to help you stay active while recovering from the previous day’s workout.

Programming for

Arthritis

Arthritis
•  Arthritis is a common disorder that affects your joints, it cause pain and 
inﬂammation, making it difﬁcult to move or stay active.
• There are many types of arthritis. Each form causes different symptoms and 
may need different treatments. 
• While arthritis usually affects older adults, it can develop in men, women and 
children of any age.
• Arthritis is most common in the following areas of the body:
o Feet
o Hands
o Hips
o Knees
o Lower back

Types of Arthritis
The most common types of arthritis include:
• Osteoarthritis, or “wear and tear” arthritis: Develops when joint cartilage breaks down from 
repeated stress. It’s the most common form of arthritis
• Ankylosing spondylitis, or arthritis of the spine (usually lower back)
• Juvenile arthritis (JA), a disorder where the immune system attacks the tissue around joints. JA 
typically affects children 16 or younger.
• Gout, a disease that causes hard crystals of uric acid

• Juvenile arthritis (JA), a disorder where the immune system attacks the tissue around joints. JA 
typically affects children 16 or younger.
• Gout, a disease that causes hard crystals of uric acid formation in joints.
• Psoriatic arthritis, joint inﬂammation that develops in people with psoriasis (autoimmune 
disorder that causes skin irritation).
• Rheumatoid arthritis, a disease that causes the immune system to attack synovial membranes 
in joints.

Arthritis: how to dive into workouts 
• The key to an arthritis exercise program is to identify an exercise regimen that 
best meets an individual’s needs and expectations. 
• It must be safe, effective, and personally enjoyable. 
• As a rule, the ﬁtness professional should design an exercise program that 
includes four major types of exercise: Flexibility, Strength, Cardiovascular, 
body awareness.
• The recommendations for frequency, intensity, time, and type are similar to 
those for apparently healthy clients with some additional c

cise: Flexibility, Strength, Cardiovascular, 
body awareness.
• The recommendations for frequency, intensity, time, and type are similar to 
those for apparently healthy clients with some additional considerations

Arthritis: workout routine
• Participating in joint-friendly physical activity can improve arthritis pain, function, mood, and quality of 
life.
• Joint-friendly physical activities that are low-impact, which means they put less stress on the body, 
reducing the risk of injury.
• Some examples of joint-friendly activities include walking, biking and swimming. 
• Incorporating an adequate warm-up and cool down in the exercise plan to protect joints and decrease 
the potential of pain. 
• The warm-up and cool-down periods should be 5 to 10 minutes in length and involve slow movements 
through a joint’s range of motion.
• Adults with arthritis should aim for at least 150 minutes a week of moderate-intensity activity such as 
brisk walking or 75 minutes a week of vigorous-intens

nts 
through a joint’s range of motion.
• Adults with arthritis should aim for at least 150 minutes a week of moderate-intensity activity such as 
brisk walking or 75 minutes a week of vigorous-intensity aerobic activity, like cycling.
• Client should also aim for at least 2 days a week of activities that strengthen muscles and include 
activities to improve balance.

Cardiovascular issues

Cardiovascular issues
• India accounts for one-ﬁfth of deaths due to CV issues worldwide especially in younger 
population. 
• The results of Global Burden of Disease study state age-standardized CVD death rate of 272 per 
100000 population in India which is much higher than that of global average of 235.*
Different conditions which can lead to CV issues being:
• Narrowing of the coronary arteries that supply heart muscle with blood & oxygen as one of the 
primary factors, there are others too.
• Inﬂammatory response within arterial wall (high blood pressure, elevated LDL cholesterol, 
elevated bloo

supply heart muscle with blood & oxygen as one of the 
primary factors, there are others too.
• Inﬂammatory response within arterial wall (high blood pressure, elevated LDL cholesterol, 
elevated blood glucose, chemical agents produced from cigarettes) & deposition of lipid-rich 
plaque & calciﬁed cholesterol. 
• Heart attack result from rupture of vulnerable plaques & release of thrombotic (blood-clotting) 
substance that narrow or completely close diameter of artery.

Various types of CV issues:
• Dyslipidemia
• Coronary artery disease
• Congestive Heart failure (CHF)
• Hypertension
• Stroke
• Peripheral Vascular Disease
• Atherosclerosis

CV issues:
• Family history 
• Hypertension 
• Smoking

• Dyslipidemia

diet)

Asthma

Asthma
Asthma is a chronic inﬂammatory disorder that is characterized by variable & recurring 
symptoms like, shortness of breath, wheezing, chest tightness & coughing.
Major triggers: 
• Allergens (dust mites, mold, cockroaches, animal dander)
• Irritants (cigarette, smoke, air pollution, strong odor, sprays, pollens etc.)
• Cold air 
• Stress
• Virus
• Exercise
The recent Global Burden of Disease (GBD, 1990–2019) estimated the total burden of asthma 
in India as 34.3 million, accounting for 13.09% of the global burden.2.

Asthma: EIA (Exercise Induced Asthma)
• 80% of people with asthma experience asthma during & after physical activity.
• Occurs after ventilation of large quantities of air, especially dry, cold containing 
environmental allergens. 
• Symptoms peak 5-10 mins after person stops exercising & may last 20-30 mins.
• Clearance from physician needed, specially if suffe

of air, especially dry, cold containing 
environmental allergens. 
• Symptoms peak 5-10 mins after person stops exercising & may last 20-30 mins.
• Clearance from physician needed, specially if suffering from chronic conditions.
• A treatment plan to be shared by physician to prevent EIA.
• People with stable asthma can workout without supervision.

Asthma: workout routine
Guidelines:
• A prolonged warm-up & cool-down is essential. 
• Rescue medication present at all times. 
• Plenty of ﬂuid intake before, during & after session. 
• Understand & avoid triggers; eg. moving indoors on extremely cold / hot days 
or when pollen count / air pollution is high.
• Initial intensity to be low followed by gradual progression.
• People with asthma respond best to sessions in mid-to-late morning. 
• Coaches should understand early signs & terminate exercise if needed.
• Controlled Asthma patients can opt for cardiovascular & strength training.

Asthma: workout routine
Exercise Recommendations:
•

Coaches should understand early signs & terminate exercise if needed.
• Controlled Asthma patients can opt for cardiovascular & strength training.

Asthma: workout routine
Exercise Recommendations:
• Low to moderate exercises: walk, swim, cycle, jog / run (if well conditioned).
• Avoid exercises with higher ventilation demand.
• Intensity: Low to moderate dynamic exercises, begin easy & gradually 
progress.
• Duration: Gradual progression to 30 mins or more, extra 10 mins for warm up 
& cool down.
• Frequency: 3-5x a week; Clients with low functional capacity should prefer  
intermittent exercise (2-3x 10 mins sessions or interval training).

Spinal, Ligament injuries and 
Migraine

Spinal cord
• The spinal cord extends downward from the base of the brain. 
• It comprises nerve cells and groups of nerves that carry messages from brain to rest of the body and 
vice versa. 
• Spine has three main parts: Cervical (neck), Thoracic (chest), Lumbar (lower back).
• In most adults, the spine i

roups of nerves that carry messages from brain to rest of the body and 
vice versa. 
• Spine has three main parts: Cervical (neck), Thoracic (chest), Lumbar (lower back).
• In most adults, the spine is composed of 33 individual back bones (vertebrae); split as 8 pairs of cervical, 
5 lumbar, 12 thoracic, 5 sacral and 1 coccygeal pair of spinal nerves.
• Spine is as important as brain! 
• Conditions like spine stenosis, sciatica, degenerative disc disease, herniated discs are few of many of 
many problems with spine. Other issues like cervical, upper back pain,  kyphosis, lordosis etc. are 
amongst others.

Spinal issues: how to dive into workouts 
• Exercise can include stretching, aerobic exercises and strength training, depending on 
health and ability of the person; nothing contraindicated under healthy / recovered 
individual.
• People with spinal issues need medical clearance from doctors before stepping into 
exercising hence making condition worse. They should also workout under

ted under healthy / recovered 
individual.
• People with spinal issues need medical clearance from doctors before stepping into 
exercising hence making condition worse. They should also workout under supervision to 
be on a safer side to avoid relapse. 
• Primary workouts to focus upon strengthening the spine and back further. Exercises like 
pelvic tilt, dead-bugs, cobra pose, downward dog, superman, scapular wall slide etc. help. 
• Any kind of exercise engagement causing symptoms to worsen need to be observed and 
evaluated. 
• Avoid overly strenuous, demanding and jerky movements, jumps, hops and undue stress 
on spine and disc; heavy lifting only after getting totally recovered.

Ligament
• Ligaments are connective tissue that provide support to the organs and 
connects the bones together. 
• Ligament sprains are very common, especially in the ankle, knee, elbows, 
shoulders and other joints. 
• A sprain is a stretch or tear of a ligament. The ones most likely to get injured 
are

ether. 
• Ligament sprains are very common, especially in the ankle, knee, elbows, 
shoulders and other joints. 
• A sprain is a stretch or tear of a ligament. The ones most likely to get injured 
are the joints at ankles, knees, and wrists.
• Improving the joint movements while maintaining a good "range of motion" 
helps to avoid sprains. 
• Riding a stationary bike or extending and ﬂexing leg are some examples.

Ligament issues: how to dive into workouts 
The goal for physical therapy is to get joint working normally and safely again in as short a 
time as possible.
Rehab can be on these lines:
• Weeks 1 - 3: The major aim is to increase range of motion and move around without 
external support. Exercises can be weight training, riding an exercise bike, toe and heel 
raises etc.
• Weeks 4 – 6: Progress to exercises that are designed to stretch and ﬂex joint as far as 
possible. Major aim is being able to walk normal. Exercises like step-ups, modiﬁed lunges 
or machine based exercises

4 – 6: Progress to exercises that are designed to stretch and ﬂex joint as far as 
possible. Major aim is being able to walk normal. Exercises like step-ups, modiﬁed lunges 
or machine based exercises like leg extensions, hamstring curls, or leg press etc. can help.
• Weeks 7 – 16: The goal is to achieve full range of motion while preventing swelling and 
pain with exercising. Using machines like a treadmill, elliptical, or stair stepper or else 
swimming and outdoor biking can be good to include.

Migraine
• A neurological condition that is more often categorized with intense 
headache which can be throbbing or a dull steady ache. 
• Other symptoms being nausea, numbness, sensitivity to light and sound, 
vomiting etc. 
• Often the symptoms range from food cravings. Neck stiffness, fatigue or low 
energy and irritability.  
• While there is no deﬁnitive cause of migraine, many factors like bright light, 
severe heat, loud sounds, excessive stress etc. are some known triggers,

Migraine issues: how to dive into workouts 
• A key beneﬁt of a regular exercise routine of any type is that it relieves stress, which is 
often another trigger for migraines. Releasing this tension through activity may cut down 
on the number of migraines you get.
• Hydrate: Being short of enough ﬂuids in system can make migraine episodes often while 
working out. Staying hydrated before, during, and after exercise will combat the issue. 
• Fuel up: Starches and sugars from the foods ingested break down into glucose, also 
called blood sugar releasing energy. Exercise can make your blood sugar level go down 
while utilizing energy. 
• Start and end slowly: Take time to warm up. Then raise the intensity gradually. Give 
enough time for a cool down. Sudden or hard activity can lead to a migraine or

Types of Diet and 
Dieting Strategies

Metabolic Adaptation
• A state where the body adapts to the lower calories that is available for 
utilization. 
• Develops the tendency to burn lesser calories for the same amount of work over 
a period of time.
What metabolic adaptation does?
• A decreased energy availability for a longer time will bring down the NEAT levels.
• The hunger levels goes up eventually. 
• The whole process will further make it tough to shred weight or recomp in due 
time thereby hitting diet plateau or workout plateau on account of less 
availability to fuel the system efﬁciently to deliver output.

weight ( say 8-10kgs or more) by being on caloric deﬁcit for few weeks, our body 
starts to…”

Diet Breaks
• As the name suggests, it is a “break” from the usual “diet.”
• The dieter is kept on a higher calories, nearly at maintenance or slight surplus while one a 
diet break “by intention.”
• A break

eaks
• As the name suggests, it is a “break” from the usual “diet.”
• The dieter is kept on a higher calories, nearly at maintenance or slight surplus while one a 
diet break “by intention.”
• A break of 2 weeks after a 12 weeks diet or 6 weeks of a 24 week dieting phase is 
recommended on a cent percent adherence to programming. This break can be placed 
depending on results and outcome and is more individual focused, one size will not ﬁt all.
Underlying beneﬁts of a diet break
• Increases NEAT
• Restore appetite hormone- leptin
• Psychological beneﬁts - helps in better adherence to the diet in longer run.
Gaining  weight when on a Diet break?
• YES! There can be a slight increase in the weight, but this is on purpose to ensure the 
plateau isn’t hit in long term.

Refeeds
How is Refeed done?
• Increase in calories for 3-6 days by intention. 
• The uptake is mainly fueled from carbohydrates to give a psychological boost, more 
energy and a kind of mental reset to dive again to goal ba

one?
• Increase in calories for 3-6 days by intention. 
• The uptake is mainly fueled from carbohydrates to give a psychological boost, more 
energy and a kind of mental reset to dive again to goal based diet.
Reasons for including Refeeds in the diet
• Decreased glycogen stores due to calorie deﬁcit.
• Metabolic adaptation.
• Decreased NEAT.

Carb Cycling
• A strategy where one is put on “high carbs” for some days and “low carbs”  on other days 
of the week.
• The overall calorie intake for the a certain period / a week is even’d out this way.
Why is this done? 
• For enhancing performance at workouts and further bettering them.
• For higher food intake on speciﬁc days by intention.
• Working on body’s metabolism.

Calorie Cycling
• A strategy where one is put on “high calories” for some days and “low calories”  on other 
days of the week.
• The overall calorie intake for the a certain period / a week is even’d out this way.
Why is this done? 
• For better food relationship and making

days and “low calories”  on other 
days of the week.
• The overall calorie intake for the a certain period / a week is even’d out this way.
Why is this done? 
• For better food relationship and making diet sustainable in longer run.
• Working on body’s metabolism.
• For higher food intake on speciﬁc days by intention.

Intermittent Fasting

Intermittent Fasting
What is Intermittent Fasting?
• A dieting strategy where food is consumed in a speciﬁc 
time window strictly. This is known as feeding window.
• The other time window is called the - fasting window 
where there is absolutely no intake of food.
• The windows can be any – generally a 16:8 hour window is 
often best considered. 
Who can follow it?
• People who have erratic work hours
• People who have limited time to cook food
• People who enjoy large meals
Who should not follow it?
• People with frequent headache.
• People who face acidity issues often.
• Pregnant Women

Misconception around Intermittent Fasting
• IF helps in managing blood sugar levels independent of the calories consumed.
• IF aids in a faster weight loss.
• Intermittent fasting vs daily calorie restriction for type 2 diabetes prevention: a review of 
human ﬁndings - PubMed (nih.gov)

Ketogenic Diet

• A type of diet in which dietary fat intake is high,  
adequate in protein and low in carbohydrates is 
consumed.
• Usually used as a dieting strategy for weight loss.
• Overall calorie count still remains in deﬁcit.
• Fat becomes the primary source of energy 
eventually.
How does KETO work?
• Glucose is the primary source of energy for body.
• When carbs are low, body eventually uses the 
glycogen stores. When these glycogen stores are 
exhausted, there is a weight loss via a lot of water 
weight.
• Body then switches to gluconeogenesis and then

glycogen stores. When these glycogen stores are 
exhausted, there is a weight loss via a lot of water 
weight.
• Body then switches to gluconeogenesis and then 
Keto-body forms ketones which is used as a source of 
energy.
Ketogenic Diet

Ketogenic Diet
What type of FATS should be added on a keto diet?
• Saturated fats are fats with chemical structure which are 
have tightly packed hydrogen atoms and they are solid at 
room temperature (Eg. coconut oil, fats present in meat)
• Unsaturated fats are fats with loosely packed double bonds of 
hydrogen (Eg. Sunﬂower oil, Olive oil, Mustard oil)
• Unsaturated fats are good fats and should be included up to 
50- 70% in the diet.

with and might as well enjoy restrictive diets.

releases energy

o Build hormones

Pathway 2: Glycogenesis – Forming Glycogen 
from Glucose
• Genesis means to generate or build; 
synthesis of glycogen. 
• Glycogen formed is stored to release energy 
as and when needed.
• Glycogen is stored either in muscle (upto 
400 gms) or in liver (upto 100 gms).

Pathway 3: Glycogenolysis - Stored Glycogen 
to glucose
• Glycogenolysis: Glycogen+lysis (splitting or breakdown). This is splitting of Glycogen to 
produce Glucose.
• Glycogen phosphorylase: It is enzyme necessary for breaking down of glycogen into 
glucose.
Liver Glycogen:
• Can be used for generation of ATP.
• Can be exported to bloodstream (for brain and RBCs).
Muscle Glycogen:
• Can only be used within the muscle.
• Glycogenolysis plays an important role in the ﬁght-or-ﬂight response and the 
regulation of glucose levels in the blood.

Pathway 4: Gluconeogenesis
• Genesis of new glucose.
• If liver glycogen concentrations are low, the body has a back-up system.

Pathway 4: Gluconeogenesis
• Genesis of new glucose.
• If liver glycogen concentrations are low, the body has a back-up system.
• The liver can make glucose from non-carbohydrate compounds via Gluconeogenesis.
• Occurs during high energy demand (exercise) or period of low nutrient availability (fasting).
• Liver acts as a blood glucose monitor
4 compounds that can go through Gluconeogenesis are :
• Pyruvate from glycolysis
• Lactate from glycolysis
• Most amino acids 
• Glycerol from triglycerides

• Changing primary energy source from Glucose to ketones in absence of carbohydrates.
• Occurs on a low carb diet or medication for Diabetes Mellitus.
• Occurs in liver.
• Fatty acids in the liver oxidizes to form Ketones.
After prolonged starvation or no carb: 
• Glucagon levels increase while Insulin levels decrease.
• The body initially goes through Gluconeogenesis for 2-3 days and then Ketones are 
produced due to ox

Pathway 5: Ketogenesis

Insulin
• Insulin is secreted by Beta cells of Pancreas.
• The hormone plays an important role in maintaining homeostasis in the blood sugar levels in the body.
• Insulin is high in the body when the body is in well fed state - glycolysis, glycogenesis.
• Brings excess glucose from the bloodstream and stores it in Liver, Muscles, Adipose tissues in the 
following forms:
o Glycogen
o Fats
Role of Insulin & Glucagon in Metabolism 
Glucagon
• Secreted by alpha cells of Pancreas.
• Plays an important role in maintaining homeostasis in the blood sugar levels in the body.
• Glucagon is secreted in the body when the body is in a fasted state: gluconeogenesis, glycogenolysis, 
ketogenesis.

The science of Weight Loss: Energy Balance
Calories are king
● Energy / Calorie Balance determines whether you gain weight, lose weight  or maintain your weight.
o Calories Input = Calories Output (Maintain Weight)  
o Calories Input > Calories Output (Gain Weight)  
o Calories Input < Calories Output (Lose Weight)
● Appetite: Inﬂuenced by hormones that regulates appetite & satiety.
● Food Consumed: Inﬂuenced by availability, palatability, energy density.
● Calories Absorbed: Inﬂuenced by macro intake, food prep, age, personal micro-biome, health status.
● Psychological Factors: Inﬂuenced by stress levels, mindset, self esteem,  sleep quality.
● Other than this, our physical environment & social structure also  inﬂuences calories in.

Basics of Anatomy

Science that studies the structure of the body
Our body consists of the following systems:
● Cardiovascular System 
● Respiratory System  
● Digestive System  
● Skeletal System 
● Nervous System  
● Muscular System  
● Endocrine System
The Human Anatomy

Kinesiology refers to studying human movement; 
from physiological, biomechanical, and 
psychological perspective
● To accomplish the work humans recruit in, there is an integration of 
anatomical,  neurological & physiological systems.
● Understanding the specialized movements associated with exercise 
performance  forms the base of developing efﬁcient training modules 
while keeping functionality  in check.
● Human body works in 3 different planes of motion:
Sagittal, Frontal,  Transverse
● In each plane, several different movements occur at the joints.
Kinesiology

As a coach, one objective is to improve client’s movement  efﬁciency and ability to perform 
activities in daily living (ADL)  thereby making them mor

erent movements occur at the joints.
Kinesiology

As a coach, one objective is to improve client’s movement  efﬁciency and ability to perform 
activities in daily living (ADL)  thereby making them more functional.
Any movement is a result of force exerted by the muscle to  perform; this action at one 
body segment then moves along the  kinetic chain.
The ability of an individual to move efﬁciently requires body to  possess appropriate levels 
of stability and mobility.
The body and its major joints follow a stable/mobile pattern  when ascending from feet to 
head.
Understanding Movement

Mobility and stability of kinetic chain

FLEXION & EXTENSION
● Flexion is a movement that decreases the angle of the joint and brings 2 
bones closer together.
● Extension is the opposite of ﬂexion, it is a movement that increases the 
angle or the distance between two bones.

away from the midline of your body.

BIOENERGETICS

● Human body’s cells require a constant supply of energy to function.
● This energy to perform comes from Food / Nutrition we ingest.
● However, the cell doesn’t directly take the energy from food.
● Body needs ATP which is the immediate usable form of chemical energy needed for all cellular functions 
(energy currency of human body).
● ATP is continuously resynthesized by using one of the three below stated energy systems, all of which are 
always  active. Their relative activities may vary from movement to movement, depending on momentary 
level of  muscular activity.
Phosphagen system
Aerobic glycolysis 
Anaerobic glycolysis
Bioenergetics of Exercise

● This system uses creatine phosphate (CP) and has a very rapid rate of ATP production.
● The total amount of CP and ATP stored in muscles is small, so there is limited energy available for 
muscular  contraction via this pathway.
● T

ate (CP) and has a very rapid rate of ATP production.
● The total amount of CP and ATP stored in muscles is small, so there is limited energy available for 
muscular  contraction via this pathway.
● This is the ﬁrst pathway to generate energy for human body demanding the release of energy
● Instantaneously available; phosphagen system is essential at the onset of activity, as well as during 
short-term  high-intensity activities lasting about 1 to 30 seconds in duration, such as 100 m sprinting, 
weight-lifting  or throwing a ball.
● Quick bouts of energy is where phosphagen system comes in action.
The Phosphagen system

Anaerobic Glycolysis
● Anaerobic simply means “without the presence of oxygen” - 
production of ATP  from Carbohydrate is Anaerobic Glycolysis 
(breakdown of glucose).
● This pathway occurs within the cytoplasm and breaks glucose down 
into a simpler  component called pyruvate to release energy for 
performing any work / activity.
● Anaerobic Glycolysis is capable of p

his pathway occurs within the cytoplasm and breaks glucose down 
into a simpler  component called pyruvate to release energy for 
performing any work / activity.
● Anaerobic Glycolysis is capable of producing ATP quite rapidly and works 
when ATP is  needed to perform activities requiring large burst of energy 
over longer periods of  time than phosphagen system will allow (30 
seconds to three minutes max, or during  endurance activities prior to 
steady state being achieved).
● Exercises like Intermittent sprinting, plyometrics, weight-lifting etc. recruit 
Anaerobic Energy Pathway more often.

● This pathway requires oxygen to produce ATP, because carbohydrates and fats are only burned in the 
presence of oxygen.
● Aerobic pathway occurs in the mitochondria of the cell and is used for activities requiring sustained energy 
production.
● Aerobic glycolysis has a slow rate of ATP production and is predominantly utilized during longer-duration, 
lower-  intensity activities after the p

ctivities requiring sustained energy 
production.
● Aerobic glycolysis has a slow rate of ATP production and is predominantly utilized during longer-duration, 
lower-  intensity activities after the phosphagen and anaerobic systems have fatigued.
● Exercises like brisk walking, swimming, running marathons, cycling etc. recruit aerobic glycolysis more often.
● It is important to remember that all three of these systems contribute to the energy needs of the body 
during  physical activity.
● These systems do not work independently of each other, but rather dominate at different times, depending 
on the duration  and the intensity of the activity.

Endurance

What is Cardiorespiratory Endurance?
● Cardio refers to cardiovascular exercise which involves repetitive, rhythmic 
movement of large muscle groups to increase heart rate and blood circulation. 
o Performed to improve cardiovascular health, burning calories, and 
increasing endurance. 
o Eg: running, cycling, swimming, jumping rope
● Vascular refers to the blood vessels in the body, including arteries, veins, and 
capillaries
o It is responsible for transporting blood, oxygen, and nutrients 
throughout the body. 
o Problems with the blood vessels can lead to conditions such as heart 
disease, stroke, and peripheral artery disease
● The respiratory system is responsible for breathing which provides oxygen to 
the body and remove carbon dioxide, a waste product of cellular metabolism.

Types of Endurance
● Cardiovascular endurance is a measure of how well a person can do exercises that involve whole body; at 
moderate to high intensity for an extended time.

Types of Endurance
● Cardiovascular endurance is a measure of how well a person can do exercises that involve whole body; at 
moderate to high intensity for an extended time.
● Cardiorespiratory endurance is the ability of the heart and lungs to provide oxygenated blood to the 
working skeletal muscles for a prolonged period without getting fatigued.
● Muscular endurance is the ability of skeletal muscles to contract sub maximally for a prolonged period 
without getting fatigued.

Muscular endurance vs Cardiorespiratory 
endurance
● Muscular endurance refers to the ability of 
a muscle or group of muscles to perform 
repeated contractions over an extended 
period of time without getting fatigued. 
● This type of endurance is speciﬁc to the 
muscles used in a particular exercise. 
● Can be improved through activities such as 
resistance training and bodyweight 
exercises.
● Cardiorespiratory endurance refers to the ability of 
the heart, lungs, and blood vessel

cular exercise. 
● Can be improved through activities such as 
resistance training and bodyweight 
exercises.
● Cardiorespiratory endurance refers to the ability of 
the heart, lungs, and blood vessels to work 
together efﬁciently during physical activity. 
● It is a measure of how well the body can supply 
oxygen to the muscles and remove waste products, 
such as carbon dioxide, during sustained exertion. 
● Cardiorespiratory endurance is improved through 
aerobic exercises, such as running, cycling, 
swimming, brisk walking which increase heart rate 
and breathing rate.
Muscular endurance focuses on the ability of speciﬁc muscles to perform repetitive contractions over 
time while cardiorespiratory endurance focuses on the overall function of the cardiovascular and 
respiratory systems during physical activity.

level

● Beginner – Low

Classiﬁcation of Intensity as per Trainee’s ﬁtness 
level
Depending on the trainee’s level in exercising, the intensity to prescribe for CV training can be: 
● Beginner – Low intensity: 50-60% max heart rate 
● Intermediate – Moderate intensity: 60-70% max heart rate 
● Advanced – High intesnity: 70-80% max heart rate 
● Super Advanced – Very high intensity: ≥ 80% max heart rate 
It is recommended to speak with a healthcare professional or exercise specialist to determine the best 
intensity for individual needs and goals.

Case study: Age : 20 yrs., RHR : 80bpm, Intensity = Beginner (50-60% MHR)
MHR method
● THR = MHR x Intensity
● THR = (220-Age) x Intensity
● THR = MHR * Intensity
● THR Lower Limit
o = (220 – Age) x 50/100
o = (220 – 20) x 50/100
o = 200 x 50/100
o = 100 bpm
● THR Upper Limit
o THR = 200 x 60/100 
o = 120 bpm
THR Range = 100-120 bpm

Karvonens formula or Heart Reserve Ratio method
● THR = HRR x Intensity+RHR 
OR
● THR = (MHR – RHR) x Intensity + RHR 
OR
● THR = (220-Age) x Intensity
● THR = [(220-age)-RHR] x Intensity + RHR
● THR = [(220-Age-RHR) x Intensity] + RHR

o = [120 x 50/100] + 80
o = 60 + 80
o = 140bpm (THR Lower Limit)
● THR = [120 x 60/100] + 80
o = 72 + 80
o = 152 bpm (THR Upper Limit)
THR Range = 140-152 bpm

Types of Cardiovascular Training
● Aerobic exercise such as running, cycling, swimming, or dancing.
● Interval training, which alternates short bursts of intense activity with 
periods of rest.
● Circuit training, which involves moving quickly from one exercise to the next 
with little rest in between.
● High-Intensity Interva

lternates short bursts of intense activity with 
periods of rest.
● Circuit training, which involves moving quickly from one exercise to the next 
with little rest in between.
● High-Intensity Interval Training (HIIT), which is a type of interval training that 
uses very intense exercise periods followed by rest.
● Endurance training, which involves longer, sustained periods of moderate to 
high-intensity exercise.

Modalities of cardiovascular workouts
● Steady State: Also known as continuous training, this involves performing low to 
moderate-intensity cardiovascular exercise for an extended period of time, such as jogging, 
cycling, or swimming. It aims at maintaining a consistent pace throughout the workout.
● Fartlek: A Swedish term meaning "speed play," this type of workout involves alternating 
periods of high-intensity exercise with periods of low-intensity recovery. The intensity and 
duration of the high-intensity periods can be varied to suit the individual's ﬁtness level an

alternating 
periods of high-intensity exercise with periods of low-intensity recovery. The intensity and 
duration of the high-intensity periods can be varied to suit the individual's ﬁtness level and 
goals.
● Interval Training: This involves repeating a set of high-intensity exercise followed by a 
period of low-intensity recovery, repeated several times over the course of the workout. The 
duration and intensity of the high-intensity periods can be varied to suit the individual's 
ﬁtness level and goals.
● Cross Training: This involves combining different types of physical activity, such as 
strength training, cardio, and stretching, into a single workout. The goal is to provide a 
comprehensive workout that challenges the cardiovascular system and improves overall 
ﬁtness.
● Circuit Training: This involves performing a series of exercises, usually with little rest in 
between, to challenge the cardiovascular system and improve muscular endurance. The 
exercises can include both ae

raining: This involves performing a series of exercises, usually with little rest in 
between, to challenge the cardiovascular system and improve muscular endurance. The 
exercises can include both aerobic and strength-training activities.

CV training Recommendations - Factors to 
consider
● Fitness level: The intensity of cardio exercise should be adjusted based on an individual's 
current ﬁtness level. Beginners may need to start with lower intensity exercises and 
gradually increase intensity over time, while more ﬁt individuals may be able to handle 
higher intensity exercises.
● Health status: Individuals with underlying health conditions such as heart disease, high 
blood pressure, or respiratory problems may need to start with lower intensity exercises and 
gradually increase intensity under the supervision of a healthcare professional.
● Age: Older individuals may need to start with lower intensity exercises and gradually 
increase intensity, as the ability to tolerate high-in

sity under the supervision of a healthcare professional.
● Age: Older individuals may need to start with lower intensity exercises and gradually 
increase intensity, as the ability to tolerate high-intensity exercise decreases with age.
● Goals: If the goal is weight loss, a higher intensity workout may be necessary, while if the 
goal is to improve overall cardiovascular health, a lower intensity workout may be sufﬁcient.
● Duration: A longer duration workout may need to be at a lower intensity to prevent injury or 
overuse, while a shorter duration workout may be able to be performed at a higher intensity.

Classiﬁcation of Aerobic activities
Aerobic activities can be classiﬁed based on the amount of joint stress they place on the body.
Low-impact aerobics: 
• Minimal stress on the joints 
• Include activities such as swimming, cycling, and using a rowing machine. 
• Often recommended for people with joint pain or those recovering from an injury.
Medium-impact aerobics: 
• Moderate s

s 
• Include activities such as swimming, cycling, and using a rowing machine. 
• Often recommended for people with joint pain or those recovering from an injury.
Medium-impact aerobics: 
• Moderate stress on the joints 
• Include activities such as walking, stair climbing, and some dance-based aerobics. 
• Provide a good balance of cardiovascular beneﬁts and joint stress.
High-impact aerobics: 
• High stress on the joints 
• Include activities such as running, jumping jacks, and plyometrics. 
• Provide a high-intensity cardiovascular workout; not recommended for people with joint pain 
or those recovering from an injury.
Consider the joint health while recommending the kind of CV activity. Individuals with joint pain 
or arthritis may need to avoid high-impact activities. However, low-impact activities may not help 
to their improve cardiovascular ﬁtness by large. In such cases, healthcare professionals should be 
considered for further advice.

The progression in cardiovascular train

act activities may not help 
to their improve cardiovascular ﬁtness by large. In such cases, healthcare professionals should be 
considered for further advice.

The progression in cardiovascular training
● Initial phase:  Starting point of cardiovascular training, where the goal is to establish a baseline level of ﬁtness 
and gradually increase intensity and duration. During this phase, exercise frequency, duration, and intensity are 
slowly increased over a period of several weeks.
● Improvement phase: Focus shifts to improving cardiovascular ﬁtness through more intense and challenging 
exercise. The intensity, duration, and frequency of exercise are gradually increased, and new challenges, such as 
interval training, are introduced.
● Maintenance phase: Final stage of cardiovascular training where the goal is to maintain the improved level of 
ﬁtness. The intensity, duration, and frequency of exercise are adjusted to maintain the desired level of ﬁtness, 
and new challenges are incor

ning where the goal is to maintain the improved level of 
ﬁtness. The intensity, duration, and frequency of exercise are adjusted to maintain the desired level of ﬁtness, 
and new challenges are incorporated to prevent boredom and maintain interest in the exercise program.

Basics of Digestion
& Metabolism: Part I

What is Nutrition?
The fundamental pillar of human life
General Nutrition (Deﬁnition): The process of obtaining food to keep us alive and 
functioning. The basic purpose of obtaining food is to provide the body with energy and 
support other processes essential for sustenance of life. 
Optimal Nutrition (Deﬁnition): Eating the right amounts of nutrients on a proper 
schedule to achieve the best performance and longest possible lifetime in good health.

What is Good Health?
As per WHO, Health is a “state of complete, physical, mental and social well being and 
not merely the absence of disease and inﬁrmity”
Internal Health:
Cholesterol
Blood sugar
Hormones  (thyroid, PCOD)
Blood pressure
Performance:
Agility
Walk / Run and climb stairs
Pick up things

How you look and feel

muscle
Healthy skin, hair and nails

Macronutrients: 
The word “macro” means “big” and hence these nutrients are required in large portions to 
achieve optimal nutrition. It is measured in terms of grams.
Carbohydrates, Fats and Proteins are the macronutrients.
Micronutrients: 
These nutrients are required in smaller quantities for our overall well-being and hence are 
called “micro” or “small” nutrients . It is measured in terms of mg.

Digestion
● It is a process wherein the food in 
broken down into smaller products 
for absorption.
● The process starts from ingestion 
and ends at excretion of waste 
products from the body.
Digestion occurs in 4 major parts:
● Mouth
● Stomach
● Small Intestine
● Large Intestine

Digestion in Mouth
• Digestion starts in the mouth by the act of chewing.
• Salivary glands release saliva which contains enzymes:
o Amylase (for carbs)
o Lipase (for fats)
• Mechanically chewed food and mixed by saliva is called Bolus. This is called as Buccal Digestion. 
• The Bolus is then sent down to the stomach through esophagus (Peristalsis). Cardiac sphincter prevents 
food reﬂux back to oesophagus.

Digestion in Stomach
● Bolus is converted to partially digested food called 
Chyme in stomach which is then emptied into small 
intestine for further breakdown.
● Muscles in stomach work upon the bolus mixing it with 
digestive juices and enzymes to convert it into chyme. 
● LES: lower esophageal sphincter
○ Separates the esophagus from  the stomach.
○ Prevents acid and food from going back to  the 
food pipe.
● Following enzymes or acids facilitate digestion in 
stomach:
○ Gastric Acid (HCL)
○ Pepsin which acts on proteins
○ Lipase

Digestion in Small Intestine
Small intestine is major site of digestion and absorption and is 
made of 3 parts:
Duodenum: 10 inches in length
• Major site of nutrient absorption.
• Stomach delivers Chyme here (mixed with HCL)
• Liver / Gallbladder deliver Bile to digest fats.
• Pancreas release:
o Pancreatic Amylase (for carbs)
o Pancreatic Lipase (for fats)
o Trypsin (hydrolysis of polypeptides into smaller peptides and 
amino acids, precursor trypsinogen activated by 
enterokinases)
Jejunum: Around 2.5 meters long
• Food after digestion (sugars, amino acids, and  fatty acids) is 
absorbed into the bloodstream
Ileum: Around 3 meters long
• Final process of absorption (Calcium, Zinc , B12

Digestion in Large Intestine
• 3 inch diameter, 1 meter in length
• The Ileum of small intestine dumps 
into cecum  part of large intestine.
• Final steps of absorption (Water, some  
vitamins, bile salts) happens in large

solid waste (faeces). 
• Large intestine is the storage site of 
fecal matter.
• Fecal matter is released through anus 
as stools.

• Salivary Glands: Produces saliva which helps in lubricating the 
food and pushing it down the oesophagus.
• Liver: Plays a very important role in the absorption of 
nutrients:

Workout Scheduling

Movement Patterns

Progression and Regression in an exercise 
program
• Planning a workout / exercise program for a client is not as simple as it gets, especially when they have progressed till a 
particular level.
• Every client has a different set of moves, strains, injury history, limitations and lifting ability which needs tweaking time to 
time to make sure the trajectory of his exercising is moving up. 
• Lifting Progression:  It means advancing a movement to increase the technical difﬁculty in a lift in order to draw a higher 
training response. This aids in hypertrophy goals and building further conﬁdence in a client.
• Lifting Regression: A different movement which is intended to give a similar training response while training in another 
way which is more ﬁt for the developmental level or current training readiness. Your client may not always have to lift more 
weight than yesterday. This programming removes fatigue while keeping hypertrophy still in accou

or the developmental level or current training readiness. Your client may not always have to lift more 
weight than yesterday. This programming removes fatigue while keeping hypertrophy still in account. If a client is not ready 
for a lift you plan, probably they need implementing regression. 
• When do you set regression?: You can see your client not able to hit proper depth in a squat, or you see a scheduled 
workout but client says he is too fatigued to take it up (CNS fatigue) or the client is unable to take a bench press higher than 
or even equal to his lift at last session. 
• Designing an exercise program should also keep in mind the aspects of CNS fatigue. CNS fatigue is higher in case of 
recruiting maximum muscle groups – majorly compound movements against  isolation. To derive quality output from 
training session, CNS fatigue built up should be kept in  mind irrespective or programming progression or regression.

Training Clients with 
Different Goals – Principles of 
Hypertrophy

Training Pyramid
• Pyramid is an organizational structure that shows the most important 
aspect of training, where the most important element is placed at bottom.
• Adherence: The only th

Hypertrophy

Training Pyramid
• Pyramid is an organizational structure that shows the most important 
aspect of training, where the most important element is placed at bottom.
• Adherence: The only thing which will yield result out of a training program 
is how much consistent you have been with the schedule. No matter how 
solid your training program is if you can’t stick to it it doesn’t matter. 
• Volume, Intensity, Frequency: Forming the base of a training split, these 
three are interrelated. The optimal combination of each will vary depending 
on your training age, goals, preferences, schedule and current ﬁtness level.
• Progression: In order to get better than before you need to apply the 
principle of Progressive overload. 
• Exercise Selection: It is purely goal driven; if you are a powerlifter your 
exercise selection will be different from a bodybuilder, and it will be 
different for a layman who is merely looking to improve his ﬁtness levels.
• Rest periods and tempo: these

a powerlifter your 
exercise selection will be different from a bodybuilder, and it will be 
different for a layman who is merely looking to improve his ﬁtness levels.
• Rest periods and tempo: these two factors are totally experienced driven; if 
you have just begun exercising your rest period will be longer than an 
experienced person, your speed at which you perform the exercise is also 
not the same as someone doing it for a longer time.

• Abbreviated as SAID, it stands for Speciﬁc Adaption to Imposed 
Demands.
• Here is an example to understand this principle:
o A person X aims at squatting more but he is totally new to lifting 
weights. He ﬁgures out his max squat, let’s say 12 repetitions in one set 
then spends the next 12 weeks doing leg extensions, leg curls, hip 
thrusts, and lunges. His squat will probably be a little better at the end of 
those 12 weeks. Let’s say it reaches 30 reps per set because now the 
muscles would simply be bigger and stronger. However, if he woul

lunges. His squat will probably be a little better at the end of 
those 12 weeks. Let’s say it reaches 30 reps per set because now the 
muscles would simply be bigger and stronger. However, if he would 
have spent those 12 weeks squatting only, even if all those muscles grew 
the exact same amount in both scenarios, he would still squat more at 
the end of 12 weeks while practicing the squat. 
• The most important aspect of practice is that it must be purposeful 
and focused. 
• Concentrating on cues that work to address the factors limiting the 
performance is essential. 
• The more similar the movement is to the skill a person is trying to learn, 
the more direct the carry-over will be. When a person is comparatively 
new to lifting, avoid using TOO much variety is a better deal before trying 
to really master the movements the person is trying to learn.

SAID Principle

• PO simply means increasing the demand from your body.
• Progressive overload can be incorporated in many ways but the 
simplest of all is adding weight on the exercise gradually in a 
planned manner.
• It works wonders for the individual who has just started working 
out.
• Number of ways in which progressive overload can be applied to 
training routine are in terms of weights a person can lift, the reps 
that he can perform or the intensity with which it is done. 
• The number of times exercise is performed (Repetitions) or the 
speed with which the exercise is done is another factor.
• Yet another factor can be time taken to do the same exercise in a 
designed time frame.
Progressive Overload

• It’s the art of organizing training into periods.
• These periods are organized in a logical order for the purpose of optimizing the 
long-term effects of training while we try to avoid injury and stagnati

the art of organizing training into periods.
• These periods are organized in a logical order for the purpose of optimizing the 
long-term effects of training while we try to avoid injury and stagnation of results.
• Single week of training is called micro cycle, longer block of training which lasts 
one or two month is called mesocycle  and the entire training plan for a given 
period or a collection of sequenced mesocycle is called macrocycle.
● Exercise periodization in most simple terms means incorporating frequency, 
volume and intensity to a workout plan to reap  maximum beneﬁts and 
gradual development in endurance, strength and power.
● This generally has 4 phases:
Hypertrophy phase  
Strength and power phase  
Peak phase 
Recovery / de-load phase
● Periodization is majorly of three types:  
Linear
Reverse linear
Non linear or Undulating periodization (DUP)
