Deload
Workout intensity in DUP will vary each week (Undulating) thereby delaying plateau and overall fatigue

• We as Coaches understood from previous slides that progressive overloading is important for making gains in 
terms of strength, muscle mass and quality of workout but there is a limit until which a person can push as per 
his max potential.
• Deloading is a phase in which the intensity / load is purposely less, or a week is taken off, or some plan to mitigate 
fatigue is emphasized.
• There are many terms for this concept like light weeks, unloads but these days it is referred as Deload.
• Simply put this is

or a week is taken off, or some plan to mitigate 
fatigue is emphasized.
• There are many terms for this concept like light weeks, unloads but these days it is referred as Deload.
• Simply put this is just a period (typically 5-7 days) where the volume of training will be less compared to your 
moderate or hard weeks of training and the intensity will be lower as well – all INTENTIONAL.
• Deloads can prove beneﬁcial because they help to reduce fatigue and thereby allow the expression of improved 
performance over a longer span of training time. 
• Accumulated fatigue is a common cause of training plateaus; but with a deload, we can expect to reduce fatigue, 
which then allows to train harder and start making progress again as a result, in the time after the deload. 
• Likewise, deloads may also reduce the risk of injury. When we train, the entire body is loaded including bones and 
connective tissues, not just the muscles. When we accumulate fatigue, we have greater risks of experienci

also reduce the risk of injury. When we train, the entire body is loaded including bones and 
connective tissues, not just the muscles. When we accumulate fatigue, we have greater risks of experiencing 
pains and injuries. The deload helps to prevent injury by allowing the connective tissues of the body to recover. 
• Finally, deloads allow often-needed mental recovery after difﬁcult training blocks and planned mesocycles.

How to read & understand 
Research Papers

An academic work that requires a person to study a particular topic 
In a research paper, the researcher forms a hypothesis, collects data and gains expert 
knowledge on that topic, they then prove or disprove the hypothesis with the data 
found through the research.
Research papers are important because they:
● Gather data & information
● Test theories 
● Provide new reliable information to negate outdated information 
● Help us understand the ‘how’ ‘why’ ‘what’ 
What is a Research Paper?

Is what you think your research will prove OR the probable answer to your research 
question
Null hypothesis: 
Claims that there is ‘no effect’ in the population
E.g: Fiber intake has no effect on constipation 
Alternate hypothesis: 
Claims that there is an effect in the population
E.g: Fiber intake has an effect on constipation
Hypothesis

• To avoid wasting money on ineffective products in the market 
• To establish fact from ﬁction for yourself & your cl

ect in the population
E.g: Fiber intake has an effect on constipation
Hypothesis

• To avoid wasting money on ineffective products in the market 
• To establish fact from ﬁction for yourself & your clients 
• To prevent myths & misconceptions from spreading 
• To assess credibility & clinical relevance 
• To know & analyse up-to-date information in your ﬁeld 
• To educate your clients, friends & family with evidence-based information 
As health coaches, why do you need to learn 
how to read Research Papers?

• Advertisements

(NCBI, NIH, examine.com, public 
library of science)

public school of health)

• Healthline, Mayo clinic

Hierarchy of Evidence

Observational vs. Experimental    
Observational Experimental
• Cross-sectional
• Case control study

tes

Hierarchy of Evidence

Observational vs. Experimental    
Observational Experimental
• Cross-sectional
• Case control study
• Cohort 
• Randomized control trials

Cross-sectional Studies   
Descriptive & Observational NOT for cause & effect relationship 
● Looks at data at a single point in time
● Mainly surveys or question
● Often used in developmental psychology, social science and education
● You can't use this to determine the cause of something such as a disease
● This type of research can be used to describe characteristics that exist in a community
● Example: researchers ﬁnd that people who reported engaging in certain health 
behaviors were also more likely to be diagnosed with speciﬁc ailments or beneﬁts
● The effect of exercising on brain health

Case - control study  
Retrospective & Observational 
● Example: Eating excessive sweets & Type 2 Diabetes 
● Deﬁnes 2 groups: 1 with the disease and 1 without 
● They as

f exercising on brain health

Case - control study  
Retrospective & Observational 
● Example: Eating excessive sweets & Type 2 Diabetes 
● Deﬁnes 2 groups: 1 with the disease and 1 without 
● They assess whether there is a difference in the rates of exposure to a deﬁned risk 
● This will suggest an association between the risk factor and development of the 
disease 
● But no deﬁnitive causality can be drawn 
RECALL BIAS

Cohort studies -   
Longitudinal & Observational 
● Example: Eating excessive sweets & Type 2 Diabetes 
● A disease free population is ﬁrst identiﬁed by the exposure (disease free but still at risk)
● They are followed in time until the disease occurs
● Since the exposure is identiﬁed before the outcome, cohort studies has the ability to 
assess causality

Randomized control trials
Experimental 
● Example: A collagen supplement for healthy hair skin and nails 
● Participants are randomly assigned different groups  (control group & treatment 
group)
● Treatment group r

rol trials
Experimental 
● Example: A collagen supplement for healthy hair skin and nails 
● Participants are randomly assigned different groups  (control group & treatment 
group)
● Treatment group receives the intervention while the control group does not 
● Lots of terminal cancer studies are done this way
● Blinded RCT

Systematic reviews & Meta-analysis of RCT’s
● Systematic Reviews: Analyses, describes and summarizes interpretations 
● Meta-analysis of RCT’s: A number of RCT’s are combined and a summary measure of 
effect is ascertained

Title
Abstract: Summary of the research (background, method, result & conclusion)
Introduction: What is currently known about the study, identify the research question & why 
it’s being studied now.  
Method/Discussion: How the study was conducted in ‘detail’ so that it can be replicated. 
Studies never stand alone, they always need to be supported by other data 
Results: The results are presented in the required format (percentages, charts, tabl

‘detail’ so that it can be replicated. 
Studies never stand alone, they always need to be supported by other data 
Results: The results are presented in the required format (percentages, charts, tables), other 
additional supporting data from other similar studies, analysis of the data & sometimes 
suggests what new hypothesis can be tested in future. 
Limitations/COI: What the researcher did not/could not consider in the research or anything 
that has gone wrong. AKA scope of improvement. 
References/Literature review: List of articles cited in the paper 
Anatomy of a Research Paper

Reading only the ‘Abstract’: Usually the authors include the details they consider to be 
most important or those that just so happen to support their hypotheses. It leaves out the 
‘context’ which can be unintentionally misleading. It does not include the limitations of the 
research as well as the analysis and relevance of the data to the real world. 
Reading only 1 or 2 papers on the topic: Topics lik

intentionally misleading. It does not include the limitations of the 
research as well as the analysis and relevance of the data to the real world. 
Reading only 1 or 2 papers on the topic: Topics like Nutrition & Exercise are complex, it 
deals with human bodies that are anatomically & medically different. It’s important to 
gather as much data as possible on a given topic..more data = more reliability & relevance. 
Assuming that it’s 100% factual because it’s a research paper: A  paper can be wrong for 
a number of reasons like an internal bias, poor design and methodology, error during data 
collection or data analysis. It’s important that you read the paper for what it is, a collection 
of data and the analysis of that data. 
Mistakes to avoid while reading research

Cancer case ascertainment

Put even more simply, lead generation is a strategy to turn visitors (from your website, social media or physical 
event) into new paying clients.

desired rep range building RIR.

Principles of Workout Programming  |
23
TIME UNDER TENSION (TUT)
It is simply the amount of time a muscle is under tension 
during a workout. This includes both the eccentric and 
concentric portions of a lift, the lengthening and shortening 
of the working muscle. E.g. bicep curls: If four bicep curls are 
performed with a two-second eccentric (downward) phase 
and a one-second concentric (upward) phase, then the biceps 
are under tension for a total of 12 seconds. If 12 such bicep 
curls are done at the same tempo, the total TUT ranges to 36 
seconds.

Principles of Workout Programming  |
24
TEMPO
Tempo training implies moving slowly and under control 
instead of moving through the reps as a general protocol or 
speed; tempo would mean breaking each movement down 
into an eccentric, concentric, and two isometric portions 
in between (one at the top and one at the bottom of the 
lift). Applying tempo training effectively works in favour of

in between (one at the top and one at the bottom of the

BILATERAL EXERCISES
Bilateral exercise trains both sides of the body 
simultaneously. Examples: squats, bench presses, deadlifts, 
bicep curls, etc. Bilateral exercises help to develop strength 
and power over the course of time, are time efficient and 
easier to execute.

ISOMETRIC EXERCISES
Isometric exercises imply tightening or contracting of a 
specific muscle or group of muscles. The muscle does not 
change length by large in an isometric exercise or hold. The 
joint also does not move.
Isometric exercises may not help to build strength but can 
help to maintain strength. They are more often recruited to 
improve the stabilization of the body or enhance it further. 
They are also important for someone who has faced an 
injury and likely needs stabili

maintain strength. They are more often recruited to 
improve the stabilization of the body or enhance it further. 
They are also important for someone who has faced an 
injury and likely needs stabilizing the joints, core, etc. Some 
examples of isometric exercises are wall sit, high plank, 
hollow hold, elbow plank, etc.

DOMS
Delayed onset muscle soreness is a pain normally reflected 
after a workout. It will not be felt during the workout. DOMS 
starts after a day or two of workout and stays for a day or two. 
As muscles tune to the intensity and threshold of the weight 
lifted, DOMS slowly starts to accommodate and does not 
affect until the intensity is further increased.
The wear and tear of muscle fibres during workouts cause 
DOMS.DOMS can affect any level of athlete - be it beginner 
or advanced. The trigger would be the intensity to which 
muscles are exposed to cause DOMS.
DOMS does not guarantee that the workout has been more 
effecti

fect any level of athlete - be it beginner 
or advanced. The trigger would be the intensity to which 
muscles are exposed to cause DOMS.
DOMS does not guarantee that the workout has been more 
effective and better. The gold standard for an effective 
workout as perceived by the old school of thought. A good 
workout can still be followed by no DOMS at all. DOMS is just 
a reflection of putting muscle fibres up against the threshold 
they are otherwise used to or trained to sustain.
DOMS gets better with time. It would not call for any 
treatment.

EPOC
Excess post-exercise oxygen consumption refers to an

30
EPOC
Excess post-exercise oxygen consumption refers to an 
increased level of oxygen the body consumes and the calories 
it burns in order to recover from working out. It would mean 
continuity to burning calories over the resting metabolic rate 
after a workout to accommodate fatigue. It is often more 
after a high-intensity workout than a low-intensity workout. 
Generally, fast-twitch muscle fibres trigger more EPOC than 
slow-twitch muscle fibres, as they generate more force and 
power. Also, they are recruited more in exercises like lifting, 
HIIT, etc. In short, the larger the muscles, the more ATP / 
energy required, the higher the EPOC.

NEAT
Non-exercise activity thermogenesis, or NEAT, means the 
calories burned by the physical movements in everyday 
activities or those that are not intended or planned for 
exercise/sports. It is also called as non-exercise physical 
activity or NEPA. NEAT may not look big, but it has a big 
impact on everyday calorie expenditure and is often the game 
changer while achieving various fitness goals. The number 
of calories an individual burns through NEAT depends on 
the amount of activity levels that person is exposed to. This 
means two individuals with the same BMI but varied activity 
levels will burn different calories via NEAT. Incorporating 
more NEAT in everyday life has been proven to make fitness 
levels better and make a person more active and fit. Small 
moves like taking stairs, not the e

different calories via NEAT. Incorporating 
more NEAT in everyday life has been proven to make fitness 
levels better and make a person more active and fit. Small 
moves like taking stairs, not the elevator, parking the car 
away from the entrance gate, walking while talking on the 
phone, a five-minute walk every hour, walking to the grocery 
store instead of taking a vehicle, etc., can make a mindful 
inclusion of NEAT easy way. These activities can burn as 
much as 10% of the total calories of BMR.

36
LOAD
A measure of the total training volume across all disciplines 
for the last 7 days is defined as training load. It can be 
measured on two parameters: duration and intensity. While 
duration is easy to account for, intensity can vary on how 
fast, how heavy or how controlled or tempo training has been 
performed throughout the week. The focus should always 
be on building the training load gradually and not scaling or 
irrationally putting it high from bottom low.
REST INTERVAL
Time spent resting between sets in order to allow the muscle 
to recover and perform the next set effectively is defined as 
a rest interval or rest period. The ideal rest period can range 
anywhere from 30 seconds to two minutes, depending on 
the type of exercise, muscle fibre targeted and inte

effectively is defined as 
a rest interval or rest period. The ideal rest period can range 
anywhere from 30 seconds to two minutes, depending on 
the type of exercise, muscle fibre targeted and intensity of 
training. Hence, the ideal rest period depends on the goal 
an individual is training for. Hypertrophy, or muscle building 
and growth, may require less rest than powerlifting as the 
intensity and load lifted, number of reps performed in each 
set and number of sets in itself are different for both training 
types.

Principles of Workout Programming  |
37
Less rest than required may cause early fatigue and make 
the total workout less effective, and the total volume achieved 
may be less than desired or targeted. A smaller than-
required rest period would fatigue muscles quickly and would 
also increase the risk of injury. On the other hand, a longer 
than required rest period would make muscles cool down and 
release tension before starting again.

TRAINING DURATION
The total time dedicated to a workout session is defined 
as the duration of the workout. There is no one-size-fits-
all approach for defining the ideal duration of a workout. It 
can range anywhere from 15 minutes to 2 hours or more 
depending on the level of training and also on the goal an 
individual is chasing. It will also revolve around the conditions 
the person is in. A full-time athlete can dedicate more hours 
to training, while a mother to 2 kids and taking care of the 
home may not be able to dedicate a lot of time each day. At 
the same time, a working professional may not dedicate a lot

of exercises again depends on factors like levels of training, 
experience in training, form and technique, history of training, 
etc. Exercise selection would also depend on how well an 
exercise is executed or performed, e.g. One individual can 
do barbell squats very effectively, while another individual 
can benefit from the same by doing a

ection would also depend on how well an 
exercise is executed or performed, e.g. One individual can 
do barbell squats very effectively, while another individual 
can benefit from the same by doing a leg press. Both these 
individuals will program their training plan accordingly.

Principles of Workout Programming  |
41
EXERCISE ORDER
The order in which one exercise is performed after the other 
is defined as exercise order. The most common thumb 
rule of exercise selection is placing compound moves first, 
which recruit fast twitch muscle fibres / larger muscle 
groups together, followed by isolation moves. This would 
mean that generally, bench press would be performed first 
in exercise order, followed by smaller muscle groups like 
triceps with tricep overhead extension or tricep rope push 
down. The reason is compound moves target large muscle 
groups, would need more energy to execute the exercise 
and also need a strong mind-muscle connection. Getting 
fatigued by performing iso

push 
down. The reason is compound moves target large muscle 
groups, would need more energy to execute the exercise 
and also need a strong mind-muscle connection. Getting 
fatigued by performing isolation or smaller muscle group 
exercises would cause body fatigue and CNS fatigue and 
will make big compound moves with lesser output than they 
could otherwise be. However, different training goals call for 
different ordering of exercises; this would depend on the level 
of training an individual is at.

Principles of Workout Programming  |
42
WHERE TO TRAIN?
All these training goals, be it hypertrophy, strength or 
power, require more dedicated and consistent efforts 
with pushing limits slowly and gradually. Hence, training 
for any of these goals would definitely demand access 
to a gym facility with a proper set-up, a good range 
of weights and equipment to program the lifts and do 
exercise variations, which can likely happen with time 
and progress. Training at home for any of th

facility with a proper set-up, a good range 
of weights and equipment to program the lifts and do 
exercise variations, which can likely happen with time 
and progress. Training at home for any of these goals is 
not possible after a limit. Once a person reaches close 
to failure in some exercise after practising it, it is not 
possible to create added resistance with body weight. 
Hence, after some time, growth will likely stall with 
home-based training in such training goals.
We also need to understand the fundamentals of training 
programming or what we can call as principles of 
training. Each program - be it hypertrophy or strength 
or power - depends on how it is scheduled, routined, or 
outlined. Let us first understand a few principles which 
make designing a workout plan in an efficient manner.

TYPES OF 
PERIODIZATION
LINEAR 
PERIODIZATION
Traditional periodization is where the trainee gradually 
decreases training volume over time and gradually increases 
training intensity. A very basic example would be like

As the number of reps reduces, the weight lifted is 
increased. This is continuously progressive. However, linear 
periodization does not address fatigue effectively as volume 
starts building up week on week.

Principles of Workout Programming  |
51
REVERSE 
PERIODIZATION
Reverse periodization is opposite to 
traditional linear periodization, where the 
start of the macrocycle is taken over intensity 
to increase power, speed, and strength 
(more weight, fewer reps). The cycle tapers 
to add more duration at a lower intensity 
(less weight, more reps).
NON LINEAR / DAILY UNDULATING 
PERIODIZATION
Non-linear periodization is a periodized 
program that is intentionally blocked into 
high-intensity low volume followed by high-
volume, lower-intensity programs. This can 
be timed within the same week or broken 
down into week blocks depending on training 
experience and availability of time.

DIFFERENCE BETWEEN LINEAR
AND NON LINEAR PERIODIZATION
Linear periodization refers to increasing specific 
variables in each workout or weekly, whereas undulating 
periodization involves cycling through variables, which 
creates an ongoing stimulus and delays fatigue to give 
long-term performance against linear adaptation. DUP 
creates muscle confusion and hence helps progress on 
a dynamic stimulus. Simple linear periodization focuses 
only on a single strength quality at a time, which rapidly 
gets lost once the programmed training is stopped, while 
DUP / non-linear periodization serves the primary focus/
goal and, at the same time, also allows the development 
of secondary

which rapidly 
gets lost once the programmed training is stopped, while 
DUP / non-linear periodization serves the primary focus/
goal and, at the same time, also allows the development 
of secondary strength qualities at the same time.
The goal of each kind of periodization is to maximize 
client performance by gradually varying the intensity, 
delaying fatigue, keeping stimulus up for a long time 
and hence improving peak potential while optimizing 
performance.

Abbreviated as SAID, it stands for Specific Adaption to 
Imposed Demands.
Here is an example to understand this principle: A person X 
aims at squatting more, but he is totally new to lifting weights. 
He figures out his max squat, let’s say 12 repetitions in one 
set, then spends the next 12 weeks doing leg extensions, leg 
curls, hip thrusts, and lunges. His squat will probably be a 
little better at the end of those 12 weeks. Let’s say it reaches 
30 reps per set because now the mu

t 12 weeks doing leg extensions, leg 
curls, hip thrusts, and lunges. His squat will probably be a 
little better at the end of those 12 weeks. Let’s say it reaches 
30 reps per set because now the muscles would simply be 
bigger and stronger. However, if he had spent those 12 weeks 
squatting only, even if all those muscles grew the exact same 
amount in both scenarios, he would still squat more at the 
end of 12 weeks while practising the squat.

DELOAD

Principles of Workout Programming  |
63
Simply put, this is just a period (typically 5-7 days) where the 
volume of training will be less compared to the moderate or 
hard weeks of training, and the intensity will be lower as well; 
all done INTENTIONAL.
Deloads can prove beneficial because they help to reduce 
fatigue and thereby allow the expression of improved 
performance over a longer span of training time.
Accumulated fatigue is a common cause of training plateaus, 
but with a deload, the fatigue is expected to reduce, which 
then allows one to train harder going forward and start 
making progress again as a result in the time after the 
deload.
Likewise, deloads may also reduce th

the fatigue is expected to reduce, which 
then allows one to train harder going forward and start 
making progress again as a result in the time after the 
deload.
Likewise, deloads may also reduce the risk of injury. The 
entire body is loaded, including bones and connective 
tissues, not just the muscles, when a proper training regime 
is followed. Accumulated fatigue warrants greater risks of 
experiencing pains and injuries way ahead. The deload helps 
to prevent injury by allowing the connective tissues of the 
body to recover.
Finally, deloads allow often-needed mental recovery after 
difficult training blocks and planned mesocycles.

TYPES OF HYPERTROPHY
MYOFIBRILLAR HYPERTROPHY
Myofibrils inside muscle fibres grow bigger, allowing muscles 
to produce more force and also developing the ability to lift 
more weight for a single repetition (1 rep max).
SARCOPLASMIC HYPERTROPHY
The sarcoplasm surrounding the myofibrils expands, giving 
more fuel and growth potential while building better work 
capacity. 
Both types eventually land in having better muscles and 
growth; heavier training stimulates myofibrillar hypertrophy 
a bit more, making an individual stronger, while high-volume 
bodybuilding produces more sarcoplasmic hypertrophy, 
giving a greater work capacity or ability to do tasks for 
a longer s

r hypertrophy 
a bit more, making an individual stronger, while high-volume 
bodybuilding produces more sarcoplasmic hypertrophy, 
giving a greater work capacity or ability to do tasks for 
a longer span of time. At the same time, Myofibrillar 
hypertrophy develops the ability to lift more weight for a 
single repetition, improving 1-rep max. On the other hand, 
Sarcoplasmic hypertrophy allows lifting weights for more 
repetitions, improving rep maxes.
The aim should be to develop both types of muscle growth 
eventually or as a person matures in training.

high-intensity low rep range zone at 85% - 
100% RM; hence, the sessions need to be 
planned depending on removing the muscle 
fatigue and the CNS fatigue. The program 
can begin by targeting three days a week 
on an alternate basis. It can eventually go 
further to 5 days a week, ensuring the loop 
is repeated after fatigue is settled. For , e.g. 
It is not recommended to squat two days 
in a row with the same intensity and same 
rep range; this will only result in delayed 
strength gains, early fatigue and high 
chances of injury going forward.
FREQUENCY

Principles of Workout Programming  |
76
When the factor of time comes into training for 
strength, the goal becomes training for power.
Power is distinct from strength. It can be defined 
as the amount of weight an individual can lift,

|
76
When the factor of time comes into training for 
strength, the goal becomes training for power.
Power is distinct from strength. It can be defined 
as the amount of weight an individual can lift, 
usually for a single or one repetition.
Power is the rate of force development; it is not 
just how much weight a person can lift but how 
fast the lift is. The muscles generate force by 
contracting, and hence, an individual is able to lift 
a weight. The ability to contract rapidly is linked to 
better strength gains and added muscle growth. 
However, improving muscle contractility, basically 
the speed at which one can explosively generate 
force with muscles, is the major focus of power 
training.
TRAINING FOR
POWER

Principles of Workout Programming  |
77
A combination of light-speed work capacity 
and heavier sets, basically high intensity 
and low reps is optimal while training for 
power . Major lifts will happen to be at 70-
90% range with 6 - 8 sets or more for all 
compound li

rk capacity 
and heavier sets, basically high intensity 
and low reps is optimal while training for 
power . Major lifts will happen to be at 70-
90% range with 6 - 8 sets or more for all 
compound lifts like squats, bench press or 
deadlifts. However , implementing a few sets 
of speed work at 40-60% a few times a week 
can accelerate the process of gaining power .
Gradually incorporating Olympic lifts in the 
program has also been proven to increase 
power . Snatch clean and jerk have been 
good tools to increase power in the lower 
body (legs or glutes) or upper body, as these 
lifts are all about explosive strength in a 
fraction of a second.
SETS, REPS & INTENSITY

Principles of Workout Programming  |
78
Power training would call for longer rest 
periods as they involve high and early CNS 
fatigue along with the major muscle groups. 
Anywhere from 4 - 8 minutes or more can 
be a good rest period between sets.
REST PERIOD
The major three lifts - squats, bench 
presses and deadlifts

fatigue along with the major muscle groups. 
Anywhere from 4 - 8 minutes or more can 
be a good rest period between sets.
REST PERIOD
The major three lifts - squats, bench 
presses and deadlifts, along with some 
plyometric work and Olympic lifts can be a 
good base to start training for power .
EXERCISE SELECTION

Principles of Workout Programming  |
79
Power training demands enough rest and 
recovery since it recruits major muscles 
with high cross-sectional area and a much 
higher CNS fatigue. The major work of 
power training can start from as much as 
twice a week and progress to alternate 
days of the week gradually with the right 
shuffling for the three major lifts.

understanding how well the recovery is and how 
much rest the individual gets and can help in

ay helps in

Good sleep is directly associated with better 
training results. Generally, 6 - 8 hours of non-
interrupted sleep is perfect to achieve. In case 
this is not possible, intermittent sleep can be 
considered as the way out.

Principles of Workout Programming  |
82
Hydration is the key. The more water an 
individual drinks, the less water the body holds 
or retains, and it makes the person feel light and 
non-bloated. Apart from this, water is the best 
detox! Keeping track of water intake is a good 
way to keep up with training goals. Generally, 3 - 
4 litres per day is a decent target.

Principles of Workout Programming  |
84
A time-to-time check of body measurements is a good 
tentative measure to track progress in a training program. 
Using a regular inch tape to check measurements like chest, 
upper abdomen, lower abdomen, hips, thigh, arms, etc., 
helps to see inch loss in due course of time while trying to 
lose fat. In the case of a planned muscle gain, the same 
measurements would see a rise gradually.

HYPERTROPHY (RIR - 6 DAYS) TRAINING PLANNER
WEEK 1 (RIR 3-4)
*1 week = 1 microcycle | 5 weeks + 1 week deload = 6 weeks = 1 mesocycle | 6 months/1 year = 1 macrocycle
This tenure can vary as per design and individualised program guidelines. Deload can be complete or 
partial depending on muscle and CNS fatigue.
PHASE 1
Every workout should start with dynamic stretching as warm up routine; this can be with few 
body weight or minimal weighted reps of an exercise for the targeted muscle group. After 
exercises, complete the routine with a full body cool down and static stretching to relax down the 
stress created on muscles and joints during workout.

PHASE 1
Every workout should start with dynamic stretching as warm up routine; this can be with few 
body weight or minimal weighted reps of an exercise for the targeted muscle group. After 
exercises, complete the routine with a full body cool down and static stretching to relax down the 
stress created on muscles and joints during workout.

How to read & understand 
Nutrition Labels

● To make you a smart, well informed & conscious consumer 
● To understand modern food
● To learn how to make nutritious decisions so that food works FOR us, not AGAINST us
● Identify food label claims 
● Learn how to read labels and ingredients list
● Understand portion sizes 
● Learn how to ask critical questions about food labels 
Learning Objectives

A display on a food item that identiﬁes the:
● Name of the product
● Quantity of product in the package
● Manufacturer details
● Ingredient list
● Nutrition facts label 
● Allergens in the food 
● Veg or non veg
This can mainly be classiﬁed into:
● Front label
● Back label
What is a nutrition label?

● The ﬁrst thing you see on the product 
● What we are drawn to like the words, phrases, pictures & colours
● Displays what is currently a trend or FAD like ‘gluten free’ or ‘high protein’
● Geared towards marketing purposes 
● What the manufacturer wants you to see
● Steer us away from something

Water, Oxygen, Alcohol 
and Fiber

Important parameters for an overall health
• Water - the best detox! Drinking water for day needs is highly under-rated and never considered by most. 
• Oxygen – because we get it so easily, have we ever realised how important it is? Ask a person who cannot 
breathe properly! 
• Fibre - nothing like good bowels everyday! 
• Alcohol - a small No can bring in greater health beneﬁts!

Water

Water – the most important and under-rated
• Often heard that drink lot of water – but why? 
• Most water intake is either through plain water or through beverages, some foods do add up a bit more 
to this. 
• An optimal intake of 3-4 litres on a daily basis (Adequate Intake – AI) for a healthy individual. 
• Generally an un-hydrated / dehydrated body can be well traced by the color of urine. A deep yellow / dark 
yellow urine often indicates higher concentration of salts and less water suggesting poor hydration 
levels. On the contrary, a light yellow color indicate

by the color of urine. A deep yellow / dark 
yellow urine often indicates higher concentration of salts and less water suggesting poor hydration 
levels. On the contrary, a light yellow color indicates decent hydration levels. 4-6 clear pees a day indicate 
reasonable hydration levels in human body.  
• Drinking water is a great practice – its zero calories, not expensive, freely available!

Role of Water in human body
Water makes a major part of our body and its cells. It is also involved in major functions like:
 
• Comprises 75% of body weight
• Flushing out body waste
• Regulating body temperature
• Helping in brain functions, enzyme actions, reactions and processes inside our body
• Major component of Saliva
• Helps to keep joint, tissues and spine healthy and lubricated
• Helps to maximize physical performance 
• Prevents constipation 
• Aids digestion, blood circulation, renal functions, skin health

Dehydration
Human body is roughly 75% water. Dehydration happens when body lose

ximize physical performance 
• Prevents constipation 
• Aids digestion, blood circulation, renal functions, skin health

Dehydration
Human body is roughly 75% water. Dehydration happens when body loses more ﬂuid than actually consumed. 
Major signs of dehydration:
• Increased thirst, dry lips & skin, feeling low and fatigued
• Low levels of energy
• Reduced urine output and often concentrated urine giving it a dark yellow color
• Muscle cramps during workouts or episodes of dizziness and headache can also occur with few
• Constipation

Overhydration
• Overhydration indicates excess of water in the body. 
• Triggers the condition called as hyponatremia (lower sodium levels in body due to excess water creating 
dilution). 
• Too much water in the cells can cause them to swell. 
• Few symptoms of overhydration being drowsiness, headache and discomfort. 
• Overhydration is common in people  whose kidneys do not excrete urine normally (slow ﬁltration rate).
Fun fact: 
• To exceed body’s abi

s of overhydration being drowsiness, headache and discomfort. 
• Overhydration is common in people  whose kidneys do not excrete urine normally (slow ﬁltration rate).
Fun fact: 
• To exceed body’s ability to excrete water and create overhydration, a young adult will have to drink more 
than 6 gallons of water everyday.

Oxygen

Oxygen
• Most vital for human body
• Almost all biochemical reactions in body would need presence of oxygen
• All body functions and brain functions cannot perform in absence of oxygen
• Oxygen uptake in body is through process or resipration and transported to body via blood 
(haemoglobin)
• What a fact: adult inhales and exhales around 7-8 litres of air / min.

Role of Oxygen 
• Oxygen is important for energy production and detoxiﬁcation
• Oxygen is taken up from blood via an enzyme monooxygenase which attaches oxygen in an atom of iron 
(haemoglobin).
• Oxygen and glucose together do the major task of producting ATP – the energy currency of cell. 
• Oxygen al

blood via an enzyme monooxygenase which attaches oxygen in an atom of iron 
(haemoglobin).
• Oxygen and glucose together do the major task of producting ATP – the energy currency of cell. 
• Oxygen also helps in clearing cellular waste (Oxygen reduction cycle)
• The capacity with which oxygen uptake is done is also an indication of health, strength and endurance
VO2 Max
• The measure of volume of oxygen that a body can consume in a minute is called as VO2 max. It is 
basically the maximum uptake possible at an increased intensity of exercising post which more uptake is 
not possible. This peak point is VO2 Max.

Alcohol

Alcohol
• Alcohol aka Ethanol (Ethyl alcohol) is made when yeast ferments the sugars in grains, 
fruits and vegetables.
• 1 gm of Alcohol has 7 Calories generally. However calories coming in from alcohol are 
often termed as “empty calories” as they barely provide any nutrition / nourishment to 
body for growth. 
• Unlike Carbs, Fat and Protein, body cannot store Alcoh

calories coming in from alcohol are 
often termed as “empty calories” as they barely provide any nutrition / nourishment to 
body for growth. 
• Unlike Carbs, Fat and Protein, body cannot store Alcohol. Hence it prioritizes alcohol 
metabolism to process and trash it out of body. The primary reason why alcohol is not a 
great choice for good health is because body uses it as primary source for energy thereby 
accumulating excess carbs and fats as adipose tissue.

Impact of Alcohol in weight loss
• Decreases Muscle protein synthesis in spite of taking enough protein
• Impairs nutrient absorption by damaging the cell lining of the stomach and intestine
• Affects sleep
• Can cause dehydration
• Poor training performance and poor recovery
• Over consumption can cause Liver damage (Alcoholic fatty liver)
• Alcohol lowers inhibitions and can lead to poor decision-making
• Affects level of hormones in body (especially testosterone)

How to build in alcohol in weight loss 
• Since alcohol consumption is often linked with poor decision making and being an addiction, at the 
same time a strong reason for piling on weight; it is important to gradually address the concern when it 
comes to fat loss. 
• It is tough for alcoholics or someone fond of drinking to give up on alcohol at day 1 of ﬁtness journey. 
• Best option is to place some drink and incorporate it in plan; gradually weaning it off in the due course 
rather than imposing a total cut off from beginning which can otherwise counteract the performance 
and ability of someone who is dependent or used to have a drink every other day. Eg. Make it 4 days to 3 
days to 2 days in the longer run. 
• Giving options of vodka, whiskey, wine, gin, tequila while avoiding soda and using diet coke instead can 
be a good start. Place a

days to 2 days in the longer run.

be a good start. Place a good meal after drinking hours.

Fiber

Dietary Fiber – A super food
● Dietary ﬁber or roughage or bulk food is the part of food which may not 
be completely broken down by human digestive enzymes and rather 
passes intact. It also may not provide energy directly but is an important 
macro in diet. 
● Found mainly in fruits, vegetables, legumes and whole grains, nuts and 
seeds. Reﬁned foods generally do have a major ﬁber content to offer. 
● important for a good gut health, clearing bowels and adding volume to 
our food while not building too many calories. Also believed to treat some 
cancers (some evidences though not individually experimented).
● Fibers are classiﬁed as Soluble or Insoluble - depending on if they can be 
dissolved in water. 
o Soluble ﬁber dissolves in water forming a

s (some evidences though not individually experimented).
● Fibers are classiﬁed as Soluble or Insoluble - depending on if they can be 
dissolved in water. 
o Soluble ﬁber dissolves in water forming a gel like material. It helps in 
lowering blood cholesterol and glucose levels. 
o Insoluble ﬁber does not dissolve in water and adds bulk to stool thereby 
making bowel movements smooth and relieve constipation.
● Daily ﬁber intake recommended in health individuals is around 25 - 35 
gms per day.

Role of ﬁber in weight loss
• Average adult should consume 25 – 35 gms of ﬁber in a day. 
• Fiber has no magical fat burning properties! 
• The only magic it does is having a good volume making the person feel full while not adding much 
calories to diet. 
• It ﬁlls the stomach due to volume thereby signaling brain to stop eating with that feeling of fullness.
• Fiber also inﬂuences the balance of healthy bacteria in the gut.
• Fiber also aids in relieving constipation when taken in the right mix

signaling brain to stop eating with that feeling of fullness.
• Fiber also inﬂuences the balance of healthy bacteria in the gut.
• Fiber also aids in relieving constipation when taken in the right mix; making bowel movements smooth. 
• People suffering from IBS should prefer consuming soluble ﬁber to avoid bloating, constipation and 
abdominal cramps.

Post-partum and 
Diastasis Recti – An 
Introduction

Understanding Postpartum?
Postpartum refers to the period beginning immediately after birth of child.
Postpartum has more deﬁnitions attached to itself: 
It also includes the period after a stillbirth (a baby who could not survive)
Postpartum phase is equally applicable to a woman who witnessed a Miscarriage:
• Miscarriage during the ﬁrst trimester (Month 1 to month 3) is more common and 
seen.
• Miscarraige can also occur during second trimester (Month 4 to month 6) is rare. 
• Miscarriage during third trimester (Month 7 to month 9) is very rare and tough.

What happens during Pregnancy?
Implantation of pregnancy to delivering a baby calls for lot of hormonal changes which a female goes 
through. 
Some important hormones involved in pregnancy and their functions are listed here:
• Placental growth factor: Promotes endothelial cell growth, placental development, and vasodilation of 
uterine vessels. 
• Human Chorionic Somatomammotrop

gnancy and their functions are listed here:
• Placental growth factor: Promotes endothelial cell growth, placental development, and vasodilation of 
uterine vessels. 
• Human Chorionic Somatomammotropin (HCS) or human placental lactogen: Modiﬁes the metabolic 
rate of mother during pregnancy to make sure of energy supply. 
• Follicle stimulating hormone (FSH): Development of eggs in women and sperm in men. 
• Luteinizing hormone (LH): Helps in sexual development, functions and also triggers release of egg from 
ovary. 
• Human chorionic gonadotropin (hCG): hormone produced by placenta – the ﬁrst marker to announce a 
pregnancy / maternal recognition with being released in urine. 
• Estrogen: increase in estrogen levels during pregnancy to maintain and grow uterus and its lining and 
also make blood ﬂow proper. 
• Progesterone: It helps uterus to grow during pregnancy and also avoid contractions during the course. It 
also helps in making body ready for lactation.  
• Relaxin: Released

ake blood ﬂow proper. 
• Progesterone: It helps uterus to grow during pregnancy and also avoid contractions during the course. It 
also helps in making body ready for lactation.  
• Relaxin: Released to relax ligaments in pelvis and soften / widen the cervix.
• Oxytocin: helps to improve contractions during labor and also to reduce bleeding post delivery. 
• Prolactin: Involved in preparing for lactation.

Phases of Postpartum Period
Three distinct but continuous phases of post partum are:
• Acute Phase: The very initial phase of 6 - 12 hours after childbirth
• Sub acute Phase: 2 - 6 Weeks after childbirth  
• Delayed Postpartum: Usually lasts up to six months after childbirth
All the phases are critical and yet most neglected phase in the lives of mothers
Most physiological and hormonal changes happen in Sub acute and delayed Postpartum  period. 
The period also reports common health problems and deﬁciencies. 
These phases are often overlooked by many females.

What happens in Postpartum Period?
• Involution: Reproductive organs returns to pre-pregnant stage – 
Shrinking or return of organ to its original size.  
• Abdominal Cramping: Uterine contractions post pregnancy to

birth to little one. 
• Breast engorgement: due to Lactation (being overfull of milk)
• Abdominal Wall: The abdominal wall remains soft & ﬂaccid post 
pregnancy. 
• Estrogen & Progesterone Levels decrease: Physiological & 
anatomical changes due to ﬂuctuation in hormone levels. 
• Changes will diﬀer in Lactating & Non-Lactating Women - this is 
depending on production of milk and supply

Postpartum Disorders
• Haemorrhage: Unusual high bleeding post pregnancy is known as post partum haemorrhage 
(Multiple reasons; primarily poor uterine contractions leading to failure in compressing the blood 
vessels attached to placenta leading them to bleed freely -  Uterine atony)
• Incontinence: Loss of bladder control – leaking urine while sneezing. Coughing, laughing or total 
inability to control urine. 
• Bacterial infections in reproductive  tract
• Mastitis: Infection in breast tissue due to blocked milk duct or secondary bacterial infections in breast.

Postpartum Disorders
• Obstetric ﬁstula: Abnormal opening between genital tract and urinary tract / rectum. The condition often 
leads to constant incontinence. Major cause is obstructed labor. 
• Perineal tearing: Area between vagina and anus is perineum. Tear happens as baby stretches the vagina 
and perineum during childbirth. 
• Pelvic organ prolapse: When one or more o

obstructed labor. 
• Perineal tearing: Area between vagina and anus is perineum. Tear happens as baby stretches the vagina 
and perineum during childbirth. 
• Pelvic organ prolapse: When one or more organs in pelvis slip down and bulge out from vagina – can be 
uterus, bladder or top of vagina. This happens when group of muscles supporting the pelvic organs are 
weakened and cannot hold organs. Prolapse can be anterior / uterine / posterior / top of vagina sagging 
down.
• Gestational Diabetes/Thyroid: High blood sugar levels during pregnancy cause GD. GT can be hyper or 
hypo – both are autoimmune  disorders called Grave’s disease and hashimoto’s disease causing over or 
subdued secretion of thyroid hormones.

Postpartum Depression (PPD)
• PPD often refers to the physical, emotional, and  behavioral 
changes that  happen in a woman after  giving birth.
• Physical Changes: a total change on how she looked and looks is 
a major depressing factor. 
• Dramatic drop of  Progesterone and es

behavioral 
changes that  happen in a woman after  giving birth.
• Physical Changes: a total change on how she looked and looks is 
a major depressing factor. 
• Dramatic drop of  Progesterone and estrogen – hormones play 
on mind.
• High Prolactin production is often considered to be another 
trigger to PPD.
• Lower Dopamine, serotonin and norepinephrine levels often 
trigger depression.
Major Symptoms:
• Depressed mood, Excessive crying
• Trouble bonding with baby
• Withdrawal from family and friends
• Loss of appetite, Loss of energy
• Insomnia or excessive sleep
• Anger, irritation, hopelessness, restlessness, fear
• Anxiety and panic attacks
• Thoughts of harming oneself and baby, death or suicide

Postpartum Alopecia

• Postpartum clients can complain of enormous hair loss.
• With postpartum alopecia, hair loss that is typically gradual, occurs  all 
over the head as more of a thinning eﬀect
• Alopecia  occurs due to hormonal ﬂuctuatio

• Alopecia  occurs due to hormonal ﬂuctuations. During pregnancy, rising 
estrogen levels slow down the natural cycle of  hair follicle shedding; 
which then fall after childbirth.

Diastasis Recti and Coning

Pelvic ﬂoor and its functions
The pelvic ﬂoor is a muscle and connective tissue that sits inside pelvis.
• It’s like hammock that provides support to the urethra, uterus, anal canal in 
women & urethra & anal canal in men. It also provides support to the baby 
during pregnancy. These muscles help to control bowel & bladder movement 
and assist with sexual function in both men and women.
• It also provides stability to deep core muscles and is responsive to changing 
intra-abdomin

muscles help to control bowel & bladder movement 
and assist with sexual function in both men and women.
• It also provides stability to deep core muscles and is responsive to changing 
intra-abdominal pressure. 
• A strong pelvic ﬂoor can help you reduce / avoid symptoms like incontinence, 
uncontrollable passing of wind, painful sex, constipation etc.
• Pelvic ﬂoor is put under severe stress during pregnancy with the weight of the 
child in womb. It is stretched out during during labor and may be  bruised & 
swollen as well.
• Return to Kegel exercises as soon as possible to bring  the pelvic ﬂoor muscles 
back to their best working  function.

Diastasis Recti
• Diastasis Recti Abdominis (DRA) is the separation of the outermost abdominal muscles. 
• It gets misinterpreted as tear in muscle; but it’s distancing of 6- pack abs from midline (connective 
tissue called linea alba).
• DRA is more evident during 2nd & 3rd trimester during pregnancy and may persist sometime post 
childbirth

cle; but it’s distancing of 6- pack abs from midline (connective 
tissue called linea alba).
• DRA is more evident during 2nd & 3rd trimester during pregnancy and may persist sometime post 
childbirth. 
• In order to accommodate the growing baby, abdomen expands, stretches skin, muscles & connective 
tissue indicating the fact that  100% women will get it by end of pregnancy. 
• The most common sign of a Diastasis Recti is a protruding belly, also known as ‘mummy tummy’ or the 
“beer belly.” 
• Many people also witness a lump or bulging out of their mid-line under load (referred as doming or 
tenting) during activities like planks, pull-ups, sit-ups etc. This bulge can be present in anyone, more 
noticeable in people with lower fat levels, and may result in abdomen that looks “still pregnant.”

Diastasis Recti: Causes
• DR may occur if core pressure isn’t managed well resulting in imbalance in pull from abs or 
pressure bulging out through midline. Thus, exaggerating separation or thin

nt.”

Diastasis Recti: Causes
• DR may occur if core pressure isn’t managed well resulting in imbalance in pull from abs or 
pressure bulging out through midline. Thus, exaggerating separation or thinning of connective 
tissue.
• Examples of such exercises: crunches, planks (if pressure not managed well), sit-ups, surya 
namaskar, kapal bhati.
• DRA maybe common postpartum injury but men, children & nulliparous get it because of 
intra-abdominal forces similar to pregnancy. 
• Coughing, laughing, pooping, breathing, moving & birthing are all things that change the 
amount of pressure in abdomen. 
• Increased intra-abdominal FAT pushing through connective tissue. 
• Poor body alignment.

Diastasis Recti: Prevention
Whether pregnant, postpartum or not, monitoring core function during exercises & activities of daily living will 
play vital role in preventing DRA. 
 
Exercising too hard or doing too much too soon postpartum can make DR worse. 
During pregnancy, postural compensations happe

es & activities of daily living will 
play vital role in preventing DRA. 
 
Exercising too hard or doing too much too soon postpartum can make DR worse. 
During pregnancy, postural compensations happen to accommodate growing belly creating imbalances 
between abdominals & pelvic position and back. These imbalances can result in poor posture thus 
contributing to DR signiﬁcantly. 
• Identifying and working on faulty breathing mechanics: work on moving muscles of the ribs, 360* expansion & 
contraction is key.
• Alignment matters: maintaining ribcage stacked over pelvic position primes core muscles to work well 
throughout the day. 
• Understanding and rectifying mobility restrictions in upper & lower body.
• Working on whole body: DR can’t be rectiﬁed with a bunch of exercises, it’s about how you breathe, hold your 
body and move that determines load on connective tissue. 
• Avoid moves that increase intra-abdominal pressure until managed well: jumping, skipping, running. These 
may cau

ow you breathe, hold your 
body and move that determines load on connective tissue. 
• Avoid moves that increase intra-abdominal pressure until managed well: jumping, skipping, running. These 
may cause bulging in linea alba or pelvic ﬂoor resulting in urine leakage issues.

Some examples of Diastasis Recti

Coning: Issue
• The center connective tissue of the abdomen, the linea alba, protrudes outwards beyond the rest of the 
abdominal wall. This tends to occur due to diastasis recti, or the normal occurring separation of the six pack 
abs during pregnancy. 
• Putting additional stress & extra strain on a weakened tissue. Coning could aggravate or further damage the 
tissue, and thus make it more challenging to heal postpartum.
Pressure Increased
• Breath holding or the Valsalva maneuver
• Exhale to bear down (exhaling down and out, as opposed to up and in)
• Sneezing, coughing, or spontaneous laughing
• Increased range of motion (picking up from the ﬂoor compared to a countertop)
• In

Exhale to bear down (exhaling down and out, as opposed to up and in)
• Sneezing, coughing, or spontaneous laughing
• Increased range of motion (picking up from the ﬂoor compared to a countertop)
• Increased loading (lifting 100lbs vs 10lbs)
Movements causing Coning
• Core ﬂexion (sit ups, crunches)
• Core isometric movements (planks)
• Overhead movements with rib thrust
• Pull Ups or hanging from bar movements
• Rowing

Coning: Solution
Postural Set Up
• Maintaining more neutral spine position during loading = better pressure management & 
prevention from coning.
• Example: Overhead movements. 
Breathing
• Focus on diaphragmatic breathing. Lengthening at lengthened position & contracting at 
contraction. Example: Squats.
• No breath hold. 
 
Engagement cues
• Main aim: turn on TA muscles to manage tension at linea alba. 
Modiﬁcation according to demand
• Tried everything & still not able to manage pressure, then think of decreasing overall 
demand on body. Decrease reps / load, change

to manage tension at linea alba. 
Modiﬁcation according to demand
• Tried everything & still not able to manage pressure, then think of decreasing overall 
demand on body. Decrease reps / load, change range of motion.

Stress Urine Incontinence 
• Stress urinary incontinence is often deﬁned as a “complaint of involuntary loss of urine on effort or physical 
exertion (eg. sporting activities), or on sneezing or coughing” 
• Examples: mostly women complain of leaking during jumping jacks, burpees, box jump or jumping on 
trampoline. 
• Almost 50 percent of the women surveyed had stress urinary incontinence and almost 100 percent had at 
least one risk factor for pelvic ﬂoor muscle dysfunction 
• Women who have never had babies are four times more likely than men to experience SUI.

Role as a Postpartum Coach
As a coach, understanding the changes during postpartum period is a very important factor. 
it is necessary to understand and ascertain the following for a postpartum client before d

Postpartum Coach
As a coach, understanding the changes during postpartum period is a very important factor. 
it is necessary to understand and ascertain the following for a postpartum client before designing a diet or 
exercise plan:
• Nature of Delivery – C-section or Vaginal?
• Underlying medical conditions during and after pregnancy
• Diastasis Recti, Incontinence, Uterine Prolapse (refer to Postpartum corrective exercise specialist / Pelvic 
Floor Therapist / Physiotherapist)
• Ascertaining any micronutrient deﬁciency
• Whether lactating or not?
• Presence of any support/Help at home during the postpartum period
• Doctor’s recommendation with regard to resuming physical activity
• Sleep cycle

Pre-pregnancy Nutrition
According to American Council of Obstetrician & Gynecology (ACOG), months before you get pregnant are the best time to take steps to be healthier. 
These steps may include:
Eating healthy diet and taking prenatal vitamins
Getting into regular exercise routine
Get mentally healthy and in that frame of mind
Work towards healthier lifestyle 
         (sleep & stress management)
Pre-pregnancy checkup
What to avoid? 
Abstaining unhealthy substances (tobacco, alcohol, marijuana, illegal drugs, and prescription drugs taken for a nonmedical reason).
Limit caffeine (1-2 cups a day)
Weight management:
Reaching and maintaining a healthy weight (A healthy weight before conceiving). Being underweight or overweight is associated with: 
Low-birth weight 
Risk of gestational diabetes
Preeclampsia
Preterm birth
Four categories of weight based on BMI:
Underweight: BMI <18.5
Normal weight: BMI between 18.5 to 24.9
O

is associated with: 
Low-birth weight 
Risk of gestational diabetes
Preeclampsia
Preterm birth
Four categories of weight based on BMI:
Underweight: BMI <18.5
Normal weight: BMI between 18.5 to 24.9
Overweight: BMI between 25 to 29.9
Obese: BMI of 30 or more

[Slide 3]
Pre-Pregnancy Care: Nutrition
Getting pregnant isn’t as easy; Weight can impact fertility by large.
Higher weight: Leading to increased fat, reduces estrogen levels which can cause irregular cycles, missed ovulation.
Low weight: Body shuts downs reproductive system to conserve energy for essential body functions 
In men, Obesity effects molecular & structural composition of sperm; can also reduce fertility.  
The key points to do:
Weight loss by cutting back on calories with balanced macros, opting for quality wholefoods and being physically active.

[Slide 10]
Breast Feeding
During pregnancy women store 2 to 5 kg (19,000 to 48,000 kcal) in tissue as fat in physiological  preparation for lactation.

Breast Milk is drawn from proteins and sugars from blood supply majorly.

Diet is one source of production of breastmilk. However if women do not consume these extra calories then body stores are used to maintain lactation.

It is not unusual for lactating women to lose 0.5-1.0 kg / month after the ﬁrst postpartum month.

Due to limited evidence, breastfeeding should therefore be promoted for its health beneﬁ

lactation.

It is not unusual for lactating women to lose 0.5-1.0 kg / month after the ﬁrst postpartum month.

Due to limited evidence, breastfeeding should therefore be promoted for its health beneﬁts for  both mother and child and not be solely relied upon as a way for women to lose weight in the  post-partum period.

Exercise does not aﬀect breast-feeding outcomes at the usual levels of activity undertaken by  mothers.

Maternal factors such as stress, anxiety, smoking etc can affect the milk production / supply. 

The quantitative value of breastmilk doesn’t change with diet and exercise.

[Slide 5]
Pre-Pregnancy Care: Exercise
Exercise is important not just because it promotes physical vigor & mental health, but helps in maintaining a healthy body weight, which improves fertility. 

Combination of aerobic & resistance training can bring in favorable changes in body weight, waist circumference & fat mass. 

Adding resistance training in exercise regime can help increase muscle mass, increase daily energy expenditure, improve lipid profile, improve insulin sensitivity. 

Combination of 20-30 minutes of moderate to high Intensity cardiovascular exercise & resistance training 3-5x a week is a good start.

ove insulin sensitivity.

[Slide 6]
Pregnancy: need for workout?
Studies indicate that women who exercise at a mild to moderate intensity for 30 minutes three or more times a week experience positive effects like:
Less physical discomfort (reduced chance of constipation, hemorrhoids, bloating, swelling, leg cramps)
Improved self-image, mood and posture
Increased energy
Better sleep
Better muscle tone, strength and endurance
Less overall body fat
Reduced risk of gestational diabetes
Reduced risk of pregnancy induced hypertension (high blood pressure)
Less time in the pushing phase of delivery (New study shows regular exercise during pregnancy shortens labor by 50 minutes)
Lower incidence of caesarean section birth
Fewer obstetric interventions (vacuum extraction, forceps)

[Slide 7]
Physiological Changes: Trim 1
HCG
Human chorionic gonadotropin (hCG) is produced at conception; 1st maternal marker (positive pregnancy).
High during early weeks of pregnancy and peaks at the 12 – 14 week mark. 
Responsible for nausea, vomiting, and fatigue in the first trimester.
To combat morning sickness, eating at regularly spaced intervals (every 2 – 4 hours) helps.
Frequent water intake and rest during workout is a MUST.
Train a first trimester client as you would train a client in the last few weeks of pregnancy
Relaxin
Relaxin hormone makes joints and ligaments less stable during pregnancy, which can put a woman at a higher risk of injury.
Exercises to be placed cautiously; avoid high impact work, hot yoga, direct core work, kickboxing, quick lateral movements and held yoga postures that place excessive strain on the shoulder or knee.
Relaxin levels d

placed cautiously; avoid high impact work, hot yoga, direct core work, kickboxing, quick lateral movements and held yoga postures that place excessive strain on the shoulder or knee.
Relaxin levels diminish post delivery, but if the joints have been overextended, the damage could be permanent.
It can take up to 5 months (or as long as the client is breastfeeding) and may result in reduced joint stability

[Slide 8]
Physiological Changes: Trim 2
Supine Hypotensive Syndrome
Discomfort with lying flat on backs for long periods of time
Can feel numbness or tingling in legs, little dizziness or lightheadedness. The baby may have enough weight to compress the vena cava (the vein returning blood to the heart) when the mother is lying on her back at this stage. 
Exercises that are normally done flat on the back can simply be done at a slightly elevated position with a stability ball or large foam wedge, exercises can also be performed in a side-lying position.
Client can perform exercises on

lat on the back can simply be done at a slightly elevated position with a stability ball or large foam wedge, exercises can also be performed in a side-lying position.
Client can perform exercises on their back, but only do a short set (90 seconds to 2 minutes) and return to sitting position then.
Preeclampsia
Complex disorder defined by the combination of hypertension and protein in urine.
Indicative preeclampsia, the female will often need to stop exercising and consult doctor before starting any.
Hypertension causes problems for the pregnant woman and her baby, and both can lead to a more serious condition called preeclampsia.
Joint Function and Synovial Fluid

Synovial Fluid lubricates the joints and assists with the body’s range of motion.
During pregnancy there is a decrease in synovial fluid production.
Joints sit more tightly together, which causes discomfort and severely limits up and down movement.
Substantial warm up of 8 to 12 minutes to allow for the joints to get fully lu

ial fluid production.
Joints sit more tightly together, which causes discomfort and severely limits up and down movement.
Substantial warm up of 8 to 12 minutes to allow for the joints to get fully lubricated with synovial fluid.

[Slide 9]
Physiological Changes: Trim 3
Sciatica (Sciatic Nerve Pain)
Shooting pains or paralyzing numbness in the lower back, buttocks area, or down the backs of the thighs. 
Primarily due to the uterus pressing on the sciatic nerve roots as they exit the spine. 
Other factors being external rotation of the hips, piriformis muscles entrapping the nerve or poor alignment.
Pelvic Girdle pain affecting pubic symphysis & SI joint:
Pain around pubic symphysis or groin
Pain in buttocks, thigh, hip or lower back 
Pain referring back of thigh to foot 
Carpal Tunnel Syndrome
Wrist pain, numbness or tingling due to fluid that compresses medial nerve
Pelvic floor Dysfunction 
Diastasis Recti

Tips to Avoid Lower Back Pain: (ACOG, 2003)
Low-heeled (but not flat) shoes with good arch support. 
Avoid lifting heavy objects; Bend at the knees to pick things up. 
Sit on chairs with good back support, or tuck a small pillow behind the lower back. 
Sleep in a side-lying position rather than on the back. 
Apply a heating pad using the lowest temperature setting, warm-water bottle, or cold compress to ease the pain.
Regular exercise in the first and second trimester and good eating habits pre-pregnancy and in early pregnancy can help clients avoid sciatic pain in the third trimester.

Post-Partum: Physiological changes
Rest & Recovery 
Education is key; your client would want to rush to pre-pregnancy weight. 
Recovery, rest & self care at this point of time is most important. 
Tissue healing itself would take 12-16 weeks (3-4 months), recovery is utmost.

Stages of healing
0 - 6 days: Inflammatory stage: swelling, heat , loss of function. 
10 days - 6 weeks: Sub-acute phase: tissue recovery.
6 -12 months: Limited ROM, muscular weakness & limited function (Connective

ing
0 - 6 days: Inflammatory stage: swelling, heat , loss of function. 
10 days - 6 weeks: Sub-acute phase: tissue recovery.
6 -12 months: Limited ROM, muscular weakness & limited function (Connective tissue strengthening & remodeling)

Recovery is individual based; it absolutely depends on tissues involved and the degree of damage caused in the procedure

[Slide 20]
Postural Assessment checklist
Pelvic tilt

Anterior pelvic tilt: 
Abdominal wall, Pelvic floor & glutes lengthening - restoring function is key
Stiffness in lower back is a common complaint

Posterior pelvic tilt: 
Flattened lumbar spine, reduced glutes, over-gripping abdominals 
Palm placement
Abdominal profile 
Rib position- flared.
Shoulder rounded
Forward head position

[Slide 21]
Self test
Measure the width (horizontally with fingers) & depth between the rectus muscles when barely lifting head.

Anything over 2.5 finger width is a Diastasis. 

It should feel like a trampoline or firm connective tissue, fingers should not sink ideally.

Self finger test is somewhat accurate, an ultrasound is gold standard.

[Slide 22]
Breathing Patterns & Functional Movements
Breathing patterns
Checking breathing patterns helps observe pressure build up. 

Ribcage & abdomen expansion should be observed during inhale & relaxation during exhale. 

Minimal movement in upper chest.
Functional movements
Observe movement patterns, specially relating to ADLs (Activities in daily living).

Any kind of pain, stiffness is to be considered while resuming workouts.

[Slide 23]
Post-partum Rehabilitation
Incredibly important; Post partum rehab supports healing process & mother’s wellbeing.
Stress is skyrocketing, but reality is different for everyone.

Postpartum exercise struggles:
Lack of support 
Difficulty adjusting to new life & baby
Mental health issues 
Exhaustion 
No motivation 
Lack of childcare options 
No. of children to look after 
Work constraints

[Slide 24]
Postpartum Rehabilitation: how to proceed
Phase 1 (Week 1 - 2)
Very short walks, rest & recover. 
Pelvic floor & core are vulnerable at this stage.

Phase 2 (Week 2 – 8)
Start moving
Posture Awareness
Deep breathing exercises
Activation & mobility exercises (core, glutes & PF)
Establish feeding 
Get rest

General Exercise recommendation 
(MUST get Dr. clearance first)
Vaginal Delivery: 4-8 weeks 
C-Section: 4-12 weeks
PHASE 3 (Week 6 to 4 months)
10-20 mins per session
Intensity: 25-50% of what the client feels really intense
Starting with intervals. 5-10 mins cardio & reassess.
Diaphragmatic Breathing & staying tall (neutral alignment)
Walking, swimming, elliptical bike with a lot of caution. 

 4 months+
Increase intensity 10% every week
Don’t increase intensity or volume in the

ragmatic Breathing & staying tall (neutral alignment)
Walking, swimming, elliptical bike with a lot of caution. 

 4 months+
Increase intensity 10% every week
Don’t increase intensity or volume in the same week same time; can result in injury.
Teach your clients: “Just because I can do it, doesn’t mean I should”
Start with 2 sets, work upto 3 - 4, then add loading
Focus to improve execution & technique
Nothing is off-limit, “modification” is the key.
